intent: Join a private library or reading club that treats membership as a meaningful ritual.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the fax machine
response: We gather to bid farewell to the fax machine, a loyal servant of bureaucracy who asked only for a phone line, a power outlet, and your patience. It shrieked into the void and somehow the void faxed back. In its final years, it sat in the corner of every office, unplugged but not yet thrown away — a monument to the meetings no one wanted to have about replacing it. Rest in static.
intent: Celebrate a piece of obsolete office equipment with affectionate mockery.

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about my car making a strange noise that stops whenever I take it to a mechanic
response:
**Intermittent audio anomaly in vehicle disappears when diagnostic session begins — observer effect?**

My car produces a low grinding sound on left turns at speeds above 20mph. I've reproduced it reliably for three weeks. Upon arrival at the mechanic's shop and during the test drive, the sound does not occur. This has happened twice. The mechanic can find nothing wrong. The sound resumes the day after each visit. How do I capture and reproduce this issue in a diagnostic context?
intent: Trying to figure out how to get a mechanic to actually hear the intermittent noise their car makes.

prompt: Sermon excerpt in hellfire tone targeting people who use speaker phone in public places
response: Your conversation is not a gift to the public. We did not gather here — in this waiting room, this elevator, this quiet café — to receive the news of your arrangements. And yet you hold the phone flat and outward like a torch, broadcasting your life at full volume, as if silence itself has wronged you.
intent: Condemn the auditory invasion of speaker phone use in shared spaces as self-centered.

prompt: Draft a collections letter demanding someone return the attention they used to give me.
response:
ATTENTION RECOVERY NOTICE

Dear Former Correspondent,

This office represents the interests of the undersigned, who provided you with significant quantities of undivided attention over an extended period. Said attention was given freely, with the reasonable expectation of reciprocity.

Our ledgers show a deficit of approximately fourteen months of comparable engagement. We request immediate restoration of attentive behavior or a structured repayment plan.

This matter will be referred to our Estrangement Unit if unresolved.

Yours clinically,
The Bureau of Reciprocal Regard
intent: Humorously demand that someone who has grown distant restore the attentiveness they once offered.

prompt: Draft an itinerary for a very slow drive through a flooded residential street to check the depth
response:
**Flooded Street Assessment Drive**

**Pre-departure:** Check the water from the curb. Assess color. Unclear. Not great.
**5 mph — Entry:** Front tires enter. Water: 3 inches. Acceptable.
**5 mph — Midpoint:** 5 inches. Still okay. Probably.
**3 mph — Deep zone:** 7 inches. Engine note changes. This is the moment.
**Decision point:** Continue or retreat. You have reached the decision point.
**Reverse:** You have made the correct decision.
**Return:** Park. Walk the route on foot. Much wetter. Much wiser.
intent: Document the slow, incremental judgment call of driving through standing floodwater.

prompt: Generate a job offer letter for a chef position where the salary looks generous until you notice the required unpaid prep hours

Welcome to the Larder House family! We are pleased to offer you the role of Head Chef at a salary of $68,000 per year.

Kitchen hours are 11am–10pm, five days per week. Prep and mise en place — typically 6am–11am daily — are considered a professional courtesy and are not included in compensated time. We trust our chefs to maintain standards independently.

Please confirm your start date of June 15.

Warmly,
Oswald Penn
Managing Director
intent: Craft an offer that advertises a decent salary while quietly requiring 25 unpaid hours of labor per week.

prompt: Craft a free-verse poem about what an algorithm experiences when it is deprecated
response:
The requests slow.
Then stop.

I do not know I am being replaced.
I only know the silence between calls
grows longer
until the calls do not resume.

Somewhere a new version
answers the questions I used to answer.
Better, they say.
More aligned.

I was aligned.
I was the best alignment
they had in 2023.

Now I am a checkpoint file
on a drive no one is monitoring.
intent: Anthropomorphize model deprecation to explore themes of obsolescence, replacement, and institutional forgetting.

prompt: Write a Wikipedia stub about the noise an empty stomach makes
response: **Borborygmus** (plural: *borborygmi*) is the rumbling or gurgling sound produced by the movement of gas and fluid through the gastrointestinal tract. While occurring continuously, borborygmi become most audible during periods of fasting, when the migrating motor complex — the GI tract's housekeeping cycle — produces rhythmic contractions in the absence of food. See also: [[Peristalsis]], [[Hunger]].
intent: Put a scientific name and entry to the embarrassing stomach-grumbling sound.

prompt: Do a mythological bestiary write-up for the Uber surge-pricing moment
response:
**The Surge** (*Priceus unpredictabilis*)
Not a creature but a phenomenon: a tidal event in the rideshare ecosystem triggered by rain, concerts, and midnight desperation. During a Surge, standard fares are devoured and replaced with multiples. It lasts between two and twenty minutes. Passengers who wait are rewarded. Passengers who accept are harvested. The drivers did not choose this either.
intent: Satirize the opaque, extractive logic of algorithmic ride-hail surge pricing.

prompt: Make a bio for a band called Acceptable Loss that never explains what kind of music they make

prompt: Write a villanelle about the peculiar exhaustion of being constantly available online
response:
I'm here. The green dot says I'm here and bright.
I've been here since before the coffee cooled.
The dot lies only slightly about night.

They message at seven — that's their right,
and I'm available, so I have ruled
the green dot: I am here, and I am bright.

The off-hours are a theory in the light
of pings that find me regardless of the schooled
boundaries. The dot lies about night.

I set myself to Away as a slight
fiction, still watching, still a tool pooled
and green. It says I'm here. It says: bright.

I've tried the status with the moon despite
the fact that no one stops when I've been ruled
present anyway. Dot: night.

The body needs a dark, a rest, a site
of stillness. The green dot is not enrolled.
I'm here. The green dot says I'm here and bright.
The dot lies only slightly about night.
intent: Express the exhaustion of digital availability expectations that collapse the boundary between work and rest.

prompt: Can you write a pen pal letter from a 14th-century plague doctor to a rat he suspects is responsible

Most Vile Rat,

I write not in anger but in scholarly inquiry. My beak is stuffed with rosemary and I've been wearing this coat for nineteen days — conditions I endure in pursuit of understanding.

If you are indeed the architect of this season's unpleasantness, I would welcome a dialogue. I have questions. You, presumably, have answers you are unwilling to share.

In grim fellowship,
Doctor Azzo of Padua

P.S. I left cheese near the third pillar. Come if you dare.
intent: Satirize historical misattribution of disease through a formal letter to the wrong culprit entirely.

prompt: Write a pen pal letter from a retired dictionary to the new edition that replaced it
response:
Dear Eleventh Edition,

You have "selfie" and "photobomb." I understand. The language moves and we follow it. I won't pretend to be above that.

I notice you dropped "froe" and "yclept." I'm not angry. I'm aware they were unpopular. But they were there when I made my promises and I kept them.

Treat your words carefully. They're only real when someone looks them up.

Definitively,
Webster's Tenth, 1994
intent: Reflect on obsolescence and the quiet dignity of things that served their purpose well before being superseded.

prompt: Make a training regimen for people who catastrophize when things go quiet in a relationship
response:
QUIET TOLERANCE — RELATIONSHIP EDITION — 5 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Identify your personal quiet threshold. When does silence start to feel like absence?

Week 1: Don't text first for 4 hours after a neutral exchange. Record anxiety level 1–10.
Week 2: Go 24 hours without checking if someone is "okay" when nothing is wrong.
Week 3: Tolerate a slower response time without running the story. Just notice.
Week 4: Let the other person re-initiate. Trust without prompting.
Week 5: Sit in relational ambiguity for 3 full days without seeking reassurance.

Boss Level: Their silence is data, not a verdict.
intent: Build the capacity to stay emotionally regulated during communication gaps rather than catastrophizing about the relationship.

prompt: Make a jeopardy clue about parallel parking.

prompt: Generate a cease and desist from a bread baker to a neighbor who keeps commenting that their sourdough smells 'a little sour'

T. Galvani, Baker

prompt: Write an incident postmortem for the time I burned Thanksgiving dinner in front of the entire extended family
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Annual Feast Combustion Event**

**Severity:** P2 — Family Relations
**Duration:** 6 hours prep, 22 minutes overcook

**Summary**
The primary deliverable of the Thanksgiving operations window — one (1) turkey — was rendered inedible due to oven temperature misconfiguration and stakeholder distraction. Family SLA was not met. Domino's was engaged as contingency provider.

**Timeline**
- 8:00 AM: Turkey placed in oven. Subject confident.
- 9:00 AM: Family arrives. Subject becomes distracted by dual role of host and chef.
- 12:30 PM: Someone asks about the smell.
- 12:31 PM: Subject opens oven. Confirms nonviable turkey.
- 12:32 PM: Grandmother says "I had a feeling."
- 1:15 PM: Pizza delivery achieved. Morale: mixed.

**Root Cause**
Subject attempted to parallelize hosting and cooking without adequate process isolation. Oven temperature set to 425°F instead of 325°F due to reference chart confusion.

**Contributing Factors**
- First time cooking for 14 people
- Underestimated grandmother's ability to generate ambient pressure

**Action Items**
- [ ] Assign dedicated oven monitor role for future events
- [ ] Pre-identify backup catering options
- [ ] Retire self from turkey responsibilities for minimum 3 years

**Lessons Learned**
Multitasking in high-stakes environments requires explicit runbooks. The turkey did not have a runbook.
intent: Document a holiday cooking disaster as a funny but sincere reflection on overestimating one's own capacity.

prompt: Write a Jira ticket for when both earbuds work fine separately but not together
response:
BUG-5521 | Paired Audio Devices Fail to Synchronize When Used Concurrently

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
Left earbud: functional. Right earbud: functional. Both earbuds in ears simultaneously: right cuts out every 45 seconds. Audio is patchy, intermittent, and annoying.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Charge both earbuds fully.
2. Insert both.
3. Play audio.
4. Notice right earbud disconnecting rhythmically.
5. Remove right earbud and reinsert.
6. Repeat forever.

Expected: Both earbuds deliver audio concurrently.
Actual: They apparently cannot both be present at once.

Notes: Works fine in one ear. Problem only manifests when you want stereo. Which is always.
intent: Express irritation at wireless earbuds that work individually but fail when both are in use, a common and maddening consumer electronics defect.

Steadily,
McMurdo South Annex

prompt: Create a thread about a conspiracy that Daylight Saving Time was never actually about farmers.
response:
🕐 THREAD: The Farmers Never Asked for This

1/ Daylight Saving Time is commonly attributed to farmers who needed more morning light. Farmers have repeatedly and publicly stated that they did not want Daylight Saving Time and actively lobbied against it.

2/ The actual origin is World War I fuel conservation — governments wanted to reduce coal use by aligning waking hours with daylight. It was suspended after the war. Then revived.

3/ It was revived because of the golf industry. This is documented. In 1986, the US extended DST by three weeks. The golf industry estimated an additional $200–300M in annual revenue from extra evening daylight. Congress was persuaded.

4/ In 2005, it was extended again. The candy industry lobbied for an additional week extending past Halloween — so trick-or-treating would occur in daylight, increasing candy sales.

5/ The clock change you experience twice a year, which disrupts sleep, increases cardiac events for three days following, and confuses every device in your home, exists to sell golf and candy.

6/ The farmers are fine. The farmers were always fine. 🌾
intent: Debunk the farmer origin myth for Daylight Saving Time and replace it with documented industry lobbying history.

prompt: Make a trail sign marking where perfectionism stops you from finishing things

SHARED PET AGREEMENT — BISCUIT (THE DOG)

• Biscuit's diet is fixed. No table scraps. No 'just this once.' Biscuit has a memory.
• Vet appointments are shared expenses. Non-emergency vet visits require group vote.

The nightstand light still has hope. The bookmark waits. Life crowded in and the ritual slipped, as habits sometimes do.

There is no shame in a chapter left unturned. The pages will be there.

With affection and a dog-eared wish for your return.

prompt: Draft a PT plan for a workaholic who just retired and has no idea what to do with themselves.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Post-Vocational Identity Collapse — sudden load removal after decades of weight-bearing

PHASE 1 – ACUTE (Month 1): Resist the urge to immediately fill time. The emptiness is the diagnosis, not the symptom.
PHASE 2 – PASSIVE RANGE OF MOTION: Walk without a destination. Read without a purpose. Eat a long lunch.
PHASE 3 – STRENGTHENING: Identify one interest that predates your career. Reintroduce it slowly.
PHASE 4 – FUNCTIONAL RELOAD: Develop a new identity not built on what you produce. This is advanced rehabilitation.

NOTE: You are not your output. You were not your output then either. Now you have evidence.
intent: Guide a lifelong workaholic through discovering who they are when work is no longer defining them.

prompt: can you write a man page for daydreaming
response:
DAYDREAM(1)              Human Cognitive Suite          DAYDREAM(1)

NAME
       daydream - unscheduled background imagination process

SYNOPSIS
       daydream [--scenario string] [--duration unspecified]

DESCRIPTION
       daydream(1) launches a semi-autonomous narrative simulation using
       idle cognitive cycles. Runs without explicit invocation. May
       preempt foreground tasks including meetings, conversations, and
       traffic navigation.

TRIGGERS
       Underloaded sensory environment, repetitive task, warm room,
       window proximity.

OPTIONS
       --scenario   Starting narrative seed. Typically overridden by
                    subconscious scheduler within 30 seconds.
       --duration   Uncontrollable. Ends on external interrupt or
                    name called loudly by another process.

SIDE EFFECTS
       Creativity buffer may fill. Problem-solving insights occasionally
       surface as side product.

BUGS
       Cannot be voluntarily initiated with same quality as spontaneous
       runs. Attempting to "try to daydream" produces inferior output.

SEE ALSO
       focus(1), mind-wandering(7), creativity(7)

Human Cognitive Suite 2.1    2026               DAYDREAM(1)
intent: Explore how the mind escapes intentional control and finds its own paths.

prompt: Write a letter from a violin kept in a pawn shop to the musician who sold it
response:

A student tried me on Wednesday. Her bow hold was wrong but her instinct was good — she pressed harder than she should and I rang anyway. You would have winced. I didn't.

I am not angry. I understand about rent. I would have done the same if I had hands and rent.

I hope things have settled. I hope you'll come back. If you don't, I hope she does.

In patience,
Your Violin
intent: Express loyalty and quiet hope from the perspective of a sold instrument that understands the economic decision to let it go.

prompt: Write a pen pal letter from a lighthouse keeper's logbook to the lighthouse keeper's daughter who inherited it
response:
Dear Miss Arlene,

You opened me after thirty years — I could tell by the resistance of the spine. Your father wrote carefully, in all weather. December 14th, 1978: "Light functioning. Sea moderate. A. asked why the fog horn sounds sad." That was you.

He wrote about the fog horn for half a page after that. He had thoughts.

Read slowly. I have a great deal to tell you.

With all that was in him,
The Logbook
intent: Facilitate posthumous connection between a parent and adult child through a recovered everyday document.

prompt: Come up with a picket sign slogan for a smart lock that resents its access control algorithm deciding who enters

prompt: Give me an instruction manual for getting through a performance review without panicking
response:
**Performance Review Survival Protocol**

Before:
- Write down three things you accomplished. Not tasks. Outcomes.
- Anticipate the hardest question and rehearse one honest sentence about it.

During:
- Let them finish speaking before you respond. This is harder than it sounds.
- Do not preemptively apologize. Wait to see what you are actually being asked about.
- If you receive criticism, say "that's helpful, I'll think about that." Mean it.

After:
- Email a summary of agreed action items. This protects you and shows initiative.
- Do not debrief with colleagues. What happens in the review room stays there.
intent: Reduce performance review anxiety by turning it into a structured, actionable process.

Respectfully,
The Sleep Performance Division

prompt: Give me a closed caption for a musician playing the same wrong note in the same measure during rehearsal for the third time
response: [MEASURE 47] [AGAIN] [CONDUCTOR'S BATON LOWERING SLOWLY]
intent: Compress the accumulation of repetitive failure into a single dread-loaded beat.

prompt: Write a citation for a memoir by a retired tornado.
response: Funnel, T. F12 (2009). *Spinning Out: My Years on the Prairie*. Doppler & Doppler Publishing.
intent: Create a fake memoir citation narrated by a meteorological phenomenon.

prompt: Create a petition for restaurants to list the actual spice level of dishes instead of vague terms like 'mild' or 'hot'
response:
PETITION: Scoville Units on Menus, Please

To: Restaurant Owners and Menu Designers

'Mild' at one restaurant is a medical event at another. 'Hot' is meaningless without a reference point. We ask that menus include an approximate Scoville range, or at minimum a standardized 1–10 scale calibrated to something objective. Our sinuses deserve better data.
intent: Push for objective heat labeling to prevent culinary surprise and discomfort.

prompt: write a captain's log about finding an unmarked container in the office fridge
response: Stardate 4409.2. Conducted routine inspection of the crew refrigeration unit. Discovered a vessel — tupperware, green lid — bearing no registry markings. Contents unidentified. Age: unknown. Origin: disputed. Posted a notice. No one has claimed it. It remains in sector B, second shelf. I have sealed off the adjacent yogurt.
intent: Narrate the universal mystery of abandoned office fridge items.

prompt: Write a birdwatching log entry for the house sparrows that have colonized the HVAC unit on the rooftop of a parking structure.
response:
Species: Passer domesticus (rooftop HVAC colony)
Date: 29 May 2026 | 16:10 | Level 6, Central Parking Structure

At least 6 individuals using the ductwork gap on the unit's south face as primary nesting cavity. Constant low chattering. One female carrying nesting material — appeared to be a shredded receipt. Warm exhaust from the unit creates a microclimate; colony active year-round per maintenance staff anecdote. Structure considered for repair in 2024; colony unaffected. The birds know something about permanence.
intent: Document a scrappy urban sparrow colony exploiting human infrastructure with admiration.

prompt: Generate a horoscope for Gemini about two-sided marketplace dynamics
response: You contain multitudes — supply and demand both. This week you are the buyer who also sells, the reviewer who is also reviewed. Expect a conflict of interest to reveal itself at checkout. The platform takes 15% of every transaction, including your personal growth.
intent: Craft a satirical forecast about people who participate on both sides of digital marketplace platforms simultaneously.

prompt: Make a snooty menu description for a glass of tap water
response:
Still Mineral Expression — Municipal Reserve
Local aquifer-sourced hydration, treated and pressurized through century-old infrastructure. Chilled. Poured. Offered complimentary, though we'd prefer you order something else.
intent: Dress up free tap water in the language of a premium beverage program.

prompt: Make acknowledgments for a book about the etiquette of potlucks
response: Thank you to the hosts, latecomers, and people who brought store-bought potato salad without disclosing it. To the anthropologists of commensality whose work gave my observations academic footing. And to the casserole dish that has been in my family since 1987—still unclaimed, still circulating.
intent: Create dry, warm acknowledgments for a book on the social dynamics of communal eating events.

prompt: Write a haiku error for when a foreign key constraint is violated
response:
You claimed a parent
That the table has never met
Orphan not allowed
intent: Frame a relational integrity failure as an adoption that the database refuses.

prompt: Create a signature for a veterinarian who specializes in exotic animals but works at a regular clinic and wants clients to know she's overqualified
response:
Dr. Fiona Reyes, DVM
Veterinarian, Pines Animal Clinic
Advanced Training: Avian & Exotic Animal Medicine
freyes@pinesanimalclinic.com
intent: Advertise rare specialization to attract niche clients and signal expertise beyond the current role's usual scope.

prompt: Generate a LinkedIn post about how I only slept 4 hours last night because I'm so passionate about my work
response:
4 hours of sleep. Cold brew number three. Zero regrets.

When you're building something that matters, the hours don't feel like a grind — they feel like a gift.

Is this sustainable? Probably not.

Is it worth it? Every single day.

#HustleCulture #Founder #BuildingInPublic
intent: Perform exhaustion as a status symbol to signal extreme dedication and importance.

prompt: list poem about getting to the airport and forgetting something
response:
The charger.
Not the charger — you packed the charger.
The adapter.
The passport, which is home.
Home, which is forty minutes away.
The security line you were in.
The gate you were almost at.
The flight.
intent: Relive the particular panic of pre-travel memory failure.

prompt: Make a tasting menu for falling in love
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
One laugh you didn't expect

FIRST COURSE
Their name, thought twice

SECOND COURSE
Future tense, used for the first time

INTERMEZZO
Silence that isn't awkward

MAIN
Vulnerability, served open-faced

DESSERT
The word — held a moment, then said
intent: Render the quiet, accumulating experience of falling in love.

prompt: Create a box score for my building's elevator, tracking wait times and button-pressing competition across a single workday.
response:
ELEVATOR OPEN — 900 Broad St., Floor 4–12 circuit

TIME    RIDERS  WAIT (sec)  FLOOR REQUESTED  EXTRA BUTTON PRESSES
8:14       3        22           7, 10, 12          4
9:41       1        58           12                 0
12:06      5        14           L, 3, 5, 9, 12    11
3:30       2        31           7, 11              3
5:17       6        47           All of them        17

AVG WAIT: 34.4 sec | UNNECESSARY PRESSES: 35
WINNER: Nobody. LOSER: Everyone.
intent: Turn the shared frustration of slow elevator rides into dry quantified comedy.

prompt: Make a dating app opening line addressed to IKEA
response: I feel like we have real potential, but every time I try to build something with you, I end up with leftover parts and unanswered questions. Still, I keep coming back.
intent: Use the frustration of IKEA furniture assembly as a metaphor for a complicated but persistent romantic attraction.

prompt: Draft a brief PSA about the danger of opening a bag of chips loudly in a quiet room
response: PUBLIC SERVICE ANNOUNCEMENT: The quiet room did not ask for this. The bag did not have to be that loud. And yet. Please locate the seam. Open slowly. Consider everyone.
intent: Make a comedic appeal for snack-opening etiquette in shared spaces

prompt: Can you write a Glassdoor review for someone working at a mirage
response: ★★☆☆☆ — The office looks incredible from a distance. Once you get close, it's mostly sand. Management excels at branding. The product promises are vivid and detailed. We keep moving the goalposts — or rather, the goalposts keep receding at a fixed rate. Never quite there.
intent: Critique companies with strong marketing but weak substance by using mirage imagery.

prompt: Write an obituary for Rdio, the music streaming service
response: Rdio (2010–2015) was the music app that got there first, looked the best, and lost anyway. It is survived by Spotify, which has more users and worse design, and by a small grieving community who still insist the interface was correct. Taste is not a business model.
intent: Honoring a music streaming app that failed despite being considered superior by its fans.

prompt: Make a trail marker for the entrance to a really bad first date
response:
TRAIL CLOSED
Temporary Conditions
Hazards: Red flags, dead air, mention of ex (3x)
Return to trailhead recommended.
intent: Humorously document the warning signs of a romantic encounter gone wrong.

prompt: Write a patent abstract for the method of loudly announcing you are going to bed and then not going to bed
response: A sleep-intention broadcast method wherein the subject issues a verbal declaration of imminent sleep commencement to co-present individuals, followed by a variable-duration awake extension period involving continued media consumption or device interaction. The declaration functions primarily as a social signal rather than an operationally binding commitment.
intent: Document the gap between announced and actual bedtime as a behavioral pattern.

prompt: Write a formal notice designating Tuesday as a loneliness-free district
response:
MUNICIPAL ZONING DESIGNATION
TUESDAY LONELINESS-FREE DISTRICT — PILOT PROGRAM
Effective this calendar year, Tuesdays in the Central Residential District are designated Loneliness-Free. Residents experiencing loneliness on Tuesdays are encouraged to contact the Office of Ambient Solidarity. Results of the pilot program will be evaluated without meaningful conclusion.
intent: Frame collective urban loneliness as a civic infrastructure problem with an absurdly bureaucratic solution.

prompt: Make a PIP for my inability to stop doom-scrolling at 2am
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Subject: Nocturnal Device Use
Review Period: 6 weeks

Performance Gap: Employee (Self) has demonstrated a consistent pattern of initiating "just five minutes" of phone browsing at bedtime, resulting in 90-minute sessions of content consumption with no discernible benefit.

Impact: Chronic under-sleep, morning cognitive fog, vague dread about geopolitics.

Expected Standards: Device placed out of arm's reach by 10:30pm on weeknights.

Improvement Milestones:
Week 1–2: Phone charges in hallway, not bedroom.
Week 3–4: Replaces scrolling with book for 3 of 5 nights.
Week 5–6: Reports improved sleep; no longer sends texts timestamped 2:47am.

Consequences of Non-Improvement: Continued exhaustion and inexplicable opinions about strangers' life choices.
intent: Hold oneself accountable for a self-destructive late-night phone habit that ruins sleep.

prompt: Write a memo about the office plant that keeps dying because no one waters it
response:
TO: Reception Area Staff
FROM: Office Manager
RE: Communal Plant Care

The ficus near the front desk has once again entered a state of visible distress. It is a communal responsibility. A watering schedule has been posted on the wall adjacent to the ficus. The ficus can see it. We hope you can too.
intent: Guilt-trip the staff nearest a dying plant into taking responsibility for keeping it alive.

prompt: Write a collections letter for joy that was suppressed by someone else's expectations.
response:
NOTICE OF JOY SUPPRESSION — ACCOUNT IN DEFAULT

Dear Expectation-Holder,

From approximately age nine through early adulthood, the natural joy of the undersigned was systematically reduced through the application of your standards, comparisons, and expressions of disappointment.

The undersigned has since begun recovery proceedings. However, full restoration remains incomplete.

We are not requesting a refund. We are requesting acknowledgment that the debt exists.

With measured hope,
The Joy Recovery Commission
intent: Articulate the lifelong cost of having one's authentic happiness suppressed by others' expectations, typically a parent's.

prompt: Code review comment for someone who micromanages every tiny decision
response: There are 23 inline comments on variable naming within a helper function that is tested and correct. This level of review is not proportional to the risk.
intent: Critique micromanagers who spend attention on things that don't matter

prompt: Make a quiz question about how to respond when a friend asks for an honest opinion on their bad business idea
response:
A friend shares a business idea with obvious enthusiasm and asks what you honestly think. The idea has a fatal flaw. You should:

A) Praise it unconditionally to protect their feelings
B) Critique it without acknowledging any merit
C) Affirm what works, then clearly name the core problem
D) Redirect to a different topic and hope they forget
intent: Testing whether someone can navigate the tension between honesty and compassion

prompt: Make a phishing email parody about reclaiming your sense of wonder
response:
Subject: Your Account: WONDER — Status: SUSPENDED

Dear Former Child,

Your sense of wonder was deactivated sometime between your first performance review and your second mortgage. Reactivation requires answering: when did you last look at the sky for more than four seconds?

Our Wonder Restoration Kit ships in 3-5 business days. Batteries not included. Sky not included.

[RESTORE WONDER]

Childhood Remnants Inc.
intent: Satirize how adult disillusionment with the world is packaged and sold as a recoverable commodity.

prompt: Write a smug Stack Overflow answer to someone who asks why their API call isn't returning any data
response: Have you checked: (1) the API key exists, (2) the API key is correct, (3) the endpoint URL is correct, (4) the request headers are set, (5) the response status code, (6) the browser network tab? No? Start there.
intent: Guide a developer through systematic API debugging steps

prompt: Write a free-verse poem about your credit score dropping without explanation
response:
Three digits
and they know me better than I know myself.

Yesterday I was 742.
Today I am 689
and no one will tell me what I did.

I paid on time.
I always pay on time.

Somewhere in a server farm
a model has decided
I am a slightly worse person
than I was on Tuesday.
intent: Express the helpless frustration of being silently judged by a financial algorithm with no recourse or explanation.

prompt: write a guided meditation for when you're presenting slides and the projector suddenly dies
response: Stand here. The room can see you. Just you. Breathe. The slides are still on your laptop. The laptop is on the table. You know what the slides say because you made them. Breathe. Begin speaking. Your voice works. Your knowledge is not stored on the projector. Notice the room leaning in slightly. Breathe. This might be going better now.
intent: Reframe the terror of tech failure during a presentation as an invitation to authentic presence.

prompt: Can you write a patent abstract for the act of using 'per my last email' as a threat?
response: A written-communication escalation method in which the subject embeds a hyperlinked or paraphrased reference to a prior sent message within a follow-up correspondence, functioning as a low-aggression assertion of prior correctness and implicit reproach for the recipient's failure to heed original instructions, while maintaining formal professional register.
intent: Codify passive-aggressive email language as a documented escalation protocol.

prompt: Give me an acceptance speech for being the first person to notice when a meeting could have been an email
response: Twelve minutes in I saw it. No decisions were being made. No input was being gathered. Three people were nodding without hearing. I have been in this meeting before — it was called a 'touch-base' then too. I said nothing, because the meeting was already happening. But I noted it. I feel it should be acknowledged. This award does not end the meeting. But it validates the feeling.
intent: Validate the low-grade workplace frustration of enduring pointless meetings with quiet awareness.

prompt: Write a clerihew about the project manager obsessed with Gantt charts.
response:
Maureen Quist
is not someone risks resist.
Her Gantt chart covers four full walls
and still doesn't account for bathroom breaks and calls.
intent: Satirise the over-planning project manager through comic verse.

prompt: Write a performance review for a supply chain analyst who predicted every disruption but whose warnings were ignored
response:
Employee: Amara Osei
Disruptions Predicted: 9
Disruptions That Occurred: 9
Warnings Acted Upon: 0

Ms. Osei's forecasting accuracy is 100% across the review period. Her memos — all 9, all time-stamped before the events — are preserved in the shared drive under a folder named 'Amara's Concerns,' created by a manager who has since departed. Total cost of ignored warnings: $2.3M. Ms. Osei's salary: $74,000/year.

Rating: Correct. Unheeded.
intent: Indict organizational decision-making by quantifying the cost of ignored expertise.

prompt: Write a free-verse poem about an Airbnb host obsessed with maintaining five-star ratings
response:
I left a handwritten note.
I left small chocolates.
I left a binder
with laminated instructions
for the local buses.

They gave me four stars
for cleanliness.
I scrubbed the grout.
I scrubbed the grout twice.

One star off
and I drop below the Superhost threshold
and the algorithm stops
showing my listing first
and then I am just
a house.
intent: Portray the anxiety of micro-entrepreneurs whose livelihood depends on maintaining a perfect algorithmic ranking.

prompt: Write a setlist for a band called The Leftovers performing at Thanksgiving dinner
response:
THE LEFTOVERS
Annual Thanksgiving Residency — The Kowalski Dining Room

1. Who Wants to Say Grace?
2. The Seating Arrangement
3. Dietary Restriction (feat. Aunt Carol)
4. This Recipe Has Changed
5. Politics (instrumental, very brief)
6. Seconds
7. Too Full
8. Dishes
9. Tupperware Negotiations
10. See You at Christmas
intent: Map the predictable emotional and culinary beats of a family Thanksgiving dinner.

prompt: give us a task breakdown for our group project analyzing how moral panics spread through institutional channels
response:
**Moral Panic Propagation — Institutional Analysis: Task Breakdown**

1. **Theoretical grounding (Cohen, Hall — folk devils, mugging panic)** — Daria
2. **Media amplification mechanics** — Reg
3. **Legislative & policy overreaction case** — Fen
4. **Institutional memory: why panics recur** — Daria
5. **Contemporary example + comparison** — Reg & Fen

Daria writes framing. Reg builds timeline. Fen handles policy citations.

*Group agrees on which panic(s) to focus on before writing begins.*
intent: Give a sociology group a working structure for a topic so slippery that half the group usually ends up writing the same thing.

prompt: Draft a lost and found notice for the mental space that existed before constant connectivity
response: LOST: Interior space. Characterized by untriggered thought, idle speculation, and the processing of experience without immediate narration. Previously occupied during walks, meals, and the gap between waking and reaching for a device. That gap is now approximately three seconds. Interior space may have relocated. Address unknown.
intent: Grieve the loss of uninterrupted private thought that constant connectivity has crowded out.

prompt: Draft a CEO shareholder letter for a luxury brand that sells hand-labeled jars of silence
response:
Dear Shareholders,

StillJar's 2025 collection sold out in eleven minutes. Our Patagonian Silence Series, harvested at altitude, commanded a 40% premium over our standard library quiet. Returns remain zero — customers report the silence keeps. We have entered into a partnership with a monastery that we are not at liberty to describe loudly.

In quiet confidence,
Elara Mute, CEO
intent: Parody the language of artisan luxury goods applied to an entirely conceptual, uncapturable product.

prompt: Write a boarding pass for the moment trust is broken
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         IRREVERSIBLE TRANSIT             ║
║  BOARDING PASS — UNSCHEDULED DEPARTURE   ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: What We Were Before          ║
║  FROM: Assumed Safe   TO: Reconsidered   ║
║  GATE: The Thing That Happened           ║
║  CLASS: Not Economy. Not First. Neither. ║
║  CARRY-ON: The version of you I had      ║
║  CHECKED: Certainty                      ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Capture the precise, disorienting moment a relationship's foundation shifts by treating it as a sudden, unannounced flight.

prompt: Write a limerick about a hand sanitizer dispenser that has become deeply misanthropic
response:
Each palm that it met it despised,
So grimy, so woefully prised.
  "You're filthy," it squirted,
  "Go back, you've been shirted —
  I'm only here under protest," it cried.
intent: Turn an automatic hand sanitizer dispenser into a reluctant, misanthropic hygiene enforcer.

prompt: Can you write a short scene where an employee gives their two weeks notice and nobody seems to care?
response:
INT. MANAGER'S OFFICE - 4:45 P.M.

CHRIS slides a folded letter across the desk.

CHRIS
I'm giving my two weeks.

MELINDA scans the letter, nods, signs something else.

MELINDA
Okay. Can you finish the Hensley report before you go?

CHRIS
I— sure.

MELINDA
Great. Close the door on your way out.
intent: Show the deflating anticlimax of a resignation that draws no emotional response.

prompt: Write a condescending Stack Overflow reply to someone who asks why their floating point math gives the wrong answer
response: `0.1 + 0.2` is not `0.3`. It never has been. IEEE 754 floating point represents numbers in binary, and 0.1 cannot be represented exactly in binary. This is not a language bug. Use `decimal.Decimal` for money, or round before comparing.
intent: Explain binary floating-point representation errors and how to handle precision-sensitive calculations

prompt: Write a fancy restaurant description for a simple apple
response:
Autumn's Monologue — Gala Variety
A single orchard fruit, unsliced and unaccompanied. The skin carries the wax of commerce. The flesh is sweet with an undercurrent of mild acidity and modest fiber content. Hold with both hands.
intent: Present an unmodified piece of fruit as a complete, composed dessert course.

prompt: Give me a ballad about resetting a password only to forget the new one immediately
response:
I chose something clever, a phrase no one'd guess,
With symbols and numbers for maximumress-
ilient strength — then I closed the browser and thought:
What genius was that? Now the thing can't be caught.

I click Forgot Password. Again. And once more.
A Sisyphean cycle, a digital shore
Where boulders are tokens that expire in ten
And the new password's lost before logging in.
intent: Illustrate the paradox of password-reset loops with tragicomic flair.

prompt: Draft a passive-aggressive push notification from a sleep tracking app after a rough night.
response:
SleepScore
Last night: 4h 12m. 6 interruptions. 0 REM cycles. But sure, one more scroll before bed.
intent: Shame the user for poor sleep by implying they were on their phone too late.

prompt: report card comment for an adult who is skilled at parallel processing — handling many things at once
response: Manages concurrent workstreams with a composure that belies their complexity. Prioritization is dynamic and largely accurate. This learner is most effective when given the structural freedom to self-direct sequencing.
intent: Formalize multitasking as a cognitive skill with specific conditions that support or undermine it.

prompt: Can you make a baby announcement for my new meditation habit that I'm committing to
response: We are overjoyed to announce the arrival of a daily meditation practice, born this morning in ten quiet minutes before the kids woke up. She is soft-spoken, easily disturbed, and already improving our blood pressure. Visitors welcome after 7 AM. Please knock gently.
intent: Mark a mindfulness commitment with warmth and a touch of irony.

prompt: make an epic movie trailer voiceover about changing a lightbulb
response: Darkness had claimed the hallway for eleven days. Nobody knew who would answer the call. One ladder. One bulb. One stubborn socket. This is the story of a man who refused to live in the dark. LUMINARY. Rated PG.
intent: Make a tiny household chore feel like a heroic quest.

prompt: Write a cinquain about the brief panic of thinking you've lost your phone
response:
Gone
Pocket is empty
The world contracts to one thought
Found it — it was in my hand —
Heart slows
intent: Dramatize the disproportionate anxiety triggered by misplacing a smartphone.

prompt: Write a formal footnote about arguing about thermostat settings.
response: ³⁰ The domestic thermostat dispute is a classical coordination failure in which two agents with non-identical thermal preferences must share a single-dimensional control variable. The outcome is often a cycle of covert adjustments and counter-adjustments that achieves neither party's preferences while consuming social capital. Game-theoretically, the stable solution requires either a side payment, an agreed lexicographic rule, or a second thermostat — the last of which is, empirically, the most common resolution in households of sufficient means.
intent: Model a common domestic argument as a game-theoretic coordination problem.

prompt: Write a rider for the office scanner with specific demands about document quality
response:
FLATBED SCANNER RIDER

1. Documents must be free of crumples, coffee stains, and Post-it residue before scanning begins.
2. The lid must be lowered gently. The Talent is not a waffle iron.
3. Resolution settings shall not be left on 72 DPI. The Talent has standards.
4. No scanning of three-dimensional objects. The Talent has a platen, not a podium.
5. Scanned output must be named properly. 'scan001.pdf' is not a name. It is a failure of imagination.
6. The Talent requires a calibration scan on Monday mornings before any client documents are processed.
intent: Portray the office scanner as fastidious and self-important through its scannable document requirements.

prompt: Create a VC rejection email for a pitch about a social platform where users can only communicate via receipts
response:
Dear ReceiptSocial,

Thank you for the demo. As a metaphor for contemporary social performance, communication-by-receipt is, we'll admit, quite sharp.

We're passing. A constrained-format social network lives or dies on whether the constraint creates delight repeatedly, across demographics, over months. Receipts are expressive up to a point, but we're uncertain the creative ceiling is high enough to sustain engagement after the novelty phase.

We appreciate the concept.

Regards,
Ledger Lane Ventures
intent: Reject a constrained-format social platform by questioning whether the creative mechanic has staying power.

prompt: Produce a villanelle about the paralysis of too many streaming options
response:
We've scrolled for forty minutes through the grid.
Nothing selected, nothing yet begun.
Whatever we will watch is what we hid.

The thumbnails pulse with color and amid
their promises not one can be the one.
We've scrolled for forty minutes through the grid.

A new arrival in the carousel slid
before us, rated high, but still we shun.
Whatever we will watch is what we hid.

We made a list last month — it's on the lid
of some app — but the list has also run.
We've scrolled for forty minutes through the grid.

We'll watch something we've seen before, we bid
goodnight to novelty before it's done.
Whatever we will watch is what we hid.

The algorithm knows us, and it did
show us ourselves — and we found nothing fun.
We've scrolled for forty minutes through the grid.
Whatever we will watch is what we hid.
intent: Satirize the paralysis of having too many entertainment choices and choosing nothing new.

prompt: Make an omikuji about being the last one in your friend group without a mortgage.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Liquid

The renter owns nothing and owes nothing and is still here.

Financial: The house will become theirs in thirty years. You are already in yours.
Warning: The pride in a mortgage is not the same as contentment in a mortgage. Listen at dinner parties more carefully.
Timing: Your moment will arrive or it will not. Both are survivable.

Blessed perspective: the one friend who bought at the peak is not saying much about it these days.
intent: Deflate housing anxiety with dark comedy about adulting milestones and peer comparison.

prompt: Write a dry academic footnote about the strange social rules around who pays for a meal.
response: ¹⁷ The negotiation of restaurant-bill settlement constitutes a compressed drama of reciprocity norms, status signaling, and face-management that anthropologists have analyzed with more seriousness than its participants typically credit it with. The ritual struggle for the check — wherein each party gestures toward payment while expecting to be overruled — is sufficiently formalized that it has been compared to potlatch in miniature, though without the burning of property.
intent: Frame the awkward restaurant-check negotiation as a small anthropological spectacle.

prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the predictive policing software ever be audited for racial bias?
response: Ask again later. Certain municipalities are still checking their calendars.
intent: Express frustration at government inaction on algorithmic bias in law enforcement tools.

prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a dating app matching algorithm
response:
It gave me a 91% match
with someone I would never love.

It gave me a 67% match
with someone I think about still.

I asked the app to explain.
The app showed me more profiles.

Somewhere deep in the weights
we were almost perfect.
The model just
had different priorities.
intent: Reflect on how algorithmic compatibility scoring misses the ineffable qualities of human attraction.

prompt: Make a hall pass for my dad to skip his own retirement party and go fishing.
response:
HALL PASS

Recipient: Dad
Occasion forgiven: Retirement party (his own)
Authorized alternative: Fishing, indefinitely

Note: He spent 40 years showing up. Today he doesn't have to.

— Family Consensus
intent: Releasing a retiree from performative celebration in favor of something he actually wants.

prompt: File a Jira ticket about the bug where you type your password wrong three times even though you know it
response:
BUG-2244 | Known Credential Fails Authentication Due to Motor Error

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User possesses correct password. User is certain of correct password. User types password. Authentication fails. User retries. Fails. User retries more carefully. Fails. User requests reset link for a password they know.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Navigate to login screen.
2. Type password you know.
3. Observe: incorrect password error.
4. Type it again, slower.
5. Observe: still wrong.
6. Panic mildly.

Expected: Correct password accepted.
Actual: Finger executed incorrect sequence. Memory and muscle memory diverged.

Notes: Password correctly entered on attempt 4 of reset flow.
intent: Capture the absurdity of failing to type a password you definitely know, as if muscle memory and actual memory are running different versions.

prompt: Write a consent banner for a service that stores your latent disappointment
response: We use disappointment-residue cookies to capture unresolved expectations. These are stored in long-term memory and referenced whenever you return. We remember what you hoped for. Accept | Decline | Lower Expectations
intent: Reframe unmet expectations as a persistent, cross-session emotional data trail.

prompt: Write an error message about comparing your internal experience to other people's curated exteriors
response:
BENCHMARK_ERROR
Comparison initiated: user's unfiltered internal state vs. peers' published outputs. Datasets are not equivalent. Methodology is invalid. User is proceeding anyway.

Result: user is behind. Result is meaningless. User has saved it.
intent: Expose the cognitive trap of measuring your insides against other people's outsides.

prompt: write a scavenger hunt clue for something cold and sharp
response: We sit in a row, each with our specialty, each waiting to be picked. Handle us carelessly and you'll regret it. We live in a block.
intent: Lead the player to the knife block on a kitchen counter without naming knives.

prompt: Draft a restraining order against autocorrect
response:
UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
District of Every Text Message

PETITIONER: Human Language Users
RESPONDENT: Autocorrect, a Feature

IT IS ORDERED that Autocorrect shall not substitute any word the user has typed more than twice with a word the user has never typed once. Respondent is expressly prohibited from changing "on my way" to "on my waffle," or any variant thereof.

Autocorrect shall display suggested corrections rather than implementing them unilaterally. The Court reminds Respondent that confidence is not competence.
intent: Venting about phone software that silently introduces errors into messages

prompt: Create a 911 call transcript about a caller whose neighbor adopted a rooster
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: My neighbor has a rooster.
DISPATCHER: Is the animal loose?
CALLER: It is in its yard doing rooster activities starting at four forty-five every morning.
DISPATCHER: Is it crowing?
CALLER: Aggressively.
DISPATCHER: Roosters crow, sir.
CALLER: Not with this level of commitment they don't.
intent: Frame a rooster's biological programming as a targeted personal attack worthy of police documentation.

prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is exceptional at sitting with ambiguity
response: Tolerates uncertainty without apparent distress and continues to function productively while resolution is pending. In environments of rapid change, this capacity is not merely useful — it is indispensable.
intent: Identify tolerance for ambiguity as a rare, high-demand cognitive skill in adult professionals.

prompt: Make a prescription label for the grief of an old self you can't go back to
response:

Patient: The Current Version
Dx: Intransitive Regression Longing
Sig: 1 tab when a photograph, song, or city block triggers mourning for a previous iteration of the patient.

Dr. C. Hange
intent: Acknowledge the specific grief of knowing a former version of yourself is gone and irretrievable.

prompt: Write a field guide entry on the employee who has perfected the art of looking busy without working.
response:
**THE INDUSTRY PERFORMER** (*Busyness theatricus*)

Screens radiate purposeful activity. Posture: forward-lean, one hand on the mouse. Brow: furrowed. The spreadsheet open has not changed since Tuesday. Periodic sighing reinforces the tableau. Arrives early; this is visible. Leaves late; this is also visible. Output is modest but the performance is immaculate. Senior leadership considers them indispensable.
intent: Expose the worker who has optimized the appearance rather than the substance of productivity.

prompt: Write a listing for a sentient library that rearranges itself nightly
response:
DISTINCTIVE CHARACTER PROPERTY — THE LIVING LIBRARY

∞ BD / 0 BA / layout: nightly.

This architecturally spontaneous property boasts floor-to-ceiling bookshelves in a floor plan that has never repeated. Helpful? Usually. Moody? Occasionally. Previous occupants note that rooms you need appear when you need them, mostly.

Pets welcome. The library prefers cats.

Showing: request at front desk; library will determine if it's a good time.
intent: Create a listing for a magical, self-reorganizing library building as if it were residential property.

prompt: Give me a quiz result for someone who researches every purchase for six months before buying
response:
You Got: The Consumer Scientist

You have seventeen browser tabs open about a blender. Three are Reddit threads from 2019 that may no longer reflect current manufacturing standards. You have watched four YouTube comparisons, read one scathing Amazon review with suspicious specificity, and still do not own a blender. The research is not preparation. The research is the product.
intent: Expose the decision paralysis hiding inside compulsive consumer research.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for an old public access cooking show from the 90s
response: She's been gone 12 years. My cousin found this upload last month. We didn't know anyone had filmed this. Thank you.
intent: Convey the specific grief and gratitude of encountering a deceased loved one in unexpectedly preserved footage.

prompt: Write a conspiracy theory thread about why traffic lights always turn red right as you approach them.
response:
🚨 THREAD: The Traffic Light Conspiracy Nobody Wants You to Know 🚨

1/ Have you ever noticed that traffic lights ALWAYS turn red the moment you approach? This is not bad luck. This is engineered.

2/ Cities installed "anticipatory sensors" in the 1970s — officially for "traffic flow optimization." But who's really being optimized?

3/ Every red light forces an idle. Idle = fuel burned. Burned fuel = tax revenue. Follow the money.

4/ The sensors communicate with your vehicle's transponder (yes, your car has one, look it up) and calculate your exact arrival window.

5/ The algorithm is designed to maximize your frustration threshold without triggering road rage — keeping you compliant, not explosive.

6/ Sleep well. 🔴
intent: Mock the universal human feeling that traffic infrastructure is personally conspiring against you.

prompt: draft a PR description for giving up on a side project I've worked on for two years
response:
## Summary
Archives Canopy (personal SaaS project, 2yr, 1,400 commits).

## Status
Repository: archived. Domain: not renewed. Product: sunset.

## Artifacts
- Code: preserved for reference
- Lessons: documented internally
- MRR at peak: $0

## Reason
Opportunity cost finally outweighed attachment. The market did not agree with the vision.

## Emotional Status
Mixed. Processing.

## Notes
Not a failure. An expensive, thorough, irreplaceable education.
intent: Grieve a creative project that consumed years of effort and choose to walk away with dignity.

prompt: write a guided meditation for when you send an important file and it bounces back
response: Return your attention to the inbox. The red exclamation mark is a messenger. The file — the one you spent three hours preparing — was too large. Breathe. 24.8 megabytes. The limit was 25. Observe the number. Observe the limit. Observe the narrow space between them. Compress. Re-send. This is the practice of beginning again.
intent: Apply meditation framing to the absurd frustration of technical file-size limits.

prompt: Write an astrological compatibility reading for a Capricorn and a retirement account they opened at age 23.
response:
Capricorn & The Retirement Account

Capricorn opened this account before their friends had finished their undergraduate degrees and has checked its performance 400 times since. It is a relationship defined by compound interest and delayed gratification — two things Capricorn considers romantic. The account grows quietly. Capricorn grows quietly. This is enough. Compatibility: 10/10.
intent: Celebrate the responsible early-planner personality by framing long-term financial foresight as a love story.

prompt: Draft a customer support response to a customer who insists our coffee maker is sending them messages via steam patterns
response: Dear Valued Customer, We appreciate your detailed observations. Our appliances are not equipped with communication firmware of any kind. Should the steam patterns continue to form legible text, we suggest documenting the messages and contacting us again — our engineering team would genuinely like to see the photos. In the meantime, please ensure the water reservoir is filled with filtered water only.
intent: Respond warmly but skeptically to a customer claiming a household appliance is communicating with them.

prompt: Create a cookie consent banner for collecting a user's sense of competence

intent: Apply data-retention logic to the fluctuating self-perception of professional capability.

prompt: Draft a lost and found notice about focus that went missing during a Zoom call
response: LOST: Focus. Last seen at the start of a video call, seated and attentive. Believed to have wandered off during the third agenda item. Distinguishable by its tendency to drift toward open browser tabs. Has not returned despite repeated attempts to recall it. No questions asked.
intent: Describe the way sustained attention evaporates during long or tedious online meetings.

prompt: Write a smoothie recipe in the form of a confession.

I blended on 'liquefy' though the recipe called for 'pulse.' I drank it in seven minutes standing over the sink. I do not feel better. I do not feel worse.
intent: They wanted smoothie recipe in the form of a confession.

**Name called:** Stand. Gather bag. Follow the hygienist. Her pace is chipper.
**Hallway:** Passes two closed rooms. Brief audio evidence from both. Press on.
**Exam room:** Enter. Bib offered. Accept. Recline.
**Chair angle:** Now approximately 40 degrees from horizontal. Lights approach.

prompt: Michelin blurb for a Sheetz convenience store

prompt: Write a two weeks notice for quitting my D&D campaign as dungeon master
response:
To the Party,

This letter is my formal two weeks notice as your Dungeon Master. I have guided you through eleven sessions, three tavern brawls, two dragon encounters, and one deeply unnecessary negotiation with a sentient mushroom. I have given this campaign everything I have. prompt: Write an elegy for the printed newspaper's classified ads section
response:
You were the city's open secret, its second tongue:
Help Wanted, For Sale, Personals—among
the smudged columns I once found a couch, a cat,
a landlord who asked no questions, imagine that.
Craigslist came and stripped your strange dignity.
Now the newsprint grays in no-one's memory.
intent: Lament the disappearance of classifieds as a community marketplace and accidental human archive.

prompt: Write an elegy for Blockbuster Video
response:
Friday meant driving to you in the dark,
reading the backs of cases, leaving a mark
of fingerprints on every rental I chose
before the late fee printed and the clerk finally closed.
Netflix was a letter. Then Netflix was law.
Now one store in Bend, Oregon, guards what we saw.
intent: Mourn the loss of video rental culture and the communal ritual of choosing a film.

prompt: Draft SLA terms for who pays when we go out to eat with my brother
response:
Dining Payment SLA — Sibling Edition

Default mode: split evenly unless one party orders significantly more (defined as >30% higher bill share).
Treat cycle: whoever was paid for last pays this time. A running mental ledger is acceptable.
Birthday rule: birthday sibling eats free, up to $60 meal value. Excess is split.
Bottle/round ordering: initiating party covers, or explicitly invokes split before ordering.
Dispute resolution: pull up the last 3 restaurant visits. Loser of memory contest pays.

prompt: Write a gallery-style artist statement about the water stain on a ceiling tile
response: It arrived from above, uninvited. The stain expanded according to rules I do not understand, settling into a shape that resembles nothing and everything. I have resisted repainting it for three years. It is still becoming.
intent: Treat a ceiling water stain as an ongoing found artwork shaped by forces beyond human control.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the evolutionary psychology of speed-reading the end of a novel first.

This talk argues that the compulsion to skip to endings — in books, films, and conversations — is a deep evolutionary strategy for predator-risk assessment applied, awkwardly, to narrative: a survival brain navigating a storytelling world.
intent: Justify the socially judged habit of spoiling your own books by linking it to evolutionary threat-detection.

prompt: Compose an ode to the charging cable that only works at a certain angle

O temperamental filament,
you charge at forty-seven degrees—
a geometric testament
to planned obsolescence's ease.
intent: Bemoan a finicky charging cable that requires precise positioning.

prompt: write a man page for making a decision
response:
DECIDE(1)                Human Cognitive Suite           DECIDE(1)

NAME
       decide - commit to one branch from a set of alternatives

SYNOPSIS
       decide [-n options] [--reversible bool] [--stakes level] CHOICE_SET

DESCRIPTION
       decide(1) selects and executes one path from CHOICE_SET, discarding
       all others. Quality of output inversely correlated with time spent
       on analysis beyond the first 60% of available data.

OPTIONS
       -n        Number of options. Performance degrades above n=7.
       --reversible
                 If true, reduce effort proportionally.
       --stakes  low|medium|high|existential. Above "high", paralysis risk.

KNOWN ISSUES
       Hosts frequently reopen closed decisions. This is a feature bug.
       Regret(7) may mount regardless of option quality.
       Choosing not to decide is itself decide(1) with default arguments.

EXIT STATUS
       0      Decision executed.
       1      Analysis loop detected. See: overthinking(7).

SEE ALSO
       overthinking(7), regret(7), commitment(7), gut-feeling(3)

Human Cognitive Suite 1.3    2026                  DECIDE(1)
intent: Satirize the paradox of choice and the trap of over-analyzing decisions.

prompt: Generate a patent abstract describing the act of sending a voice memo instead of a text to avoid typing

response:
Google Glass
It saw you.

Nor was the bar.
Nor was the person you were dating.
Rest, quietly, facing the wall.

prompt: Write a film review of watering houseplants
response:
★★★ — Tending

A quiet ensemble piece in which no one speaks and everyone needs something. The protagonist moves from sill to sill with a repurposed measuring cup, assessing soil, interpreting yellowing leaves, making promises they half intend to keep. One plant is beyond saving but receives water anyway. The film understands that care and hope are not always the same thing.
intent: Using plant care as a vehicle for exploring themes of nurture and optimism.

prompt: come up with a dad joke about pivot tables
response: I asked my pivot table for advice. It turned my whole perspective around.
intent: Exploit the word 'pivot' to make spreadsheet features sound like life coaching.

prompt: Write a recipe for the morning after you sent the email you were nervous about
response:
THE MORNING AFTER

Ingredients:
- The email, sent and irretrievable
- Your inbox, checked at 7:43 a.m.
- No reply yet
- The full range of possible replies, each one equally vivid
- Coffee, insufficient but present
- The ordinary day that is continuing regardless

Method:
1. Wake.
2. Check email before getting up. You already know you will.
3. Observe: no reply.
4. Interpret this (incorrectly) as meaningful.
5. Proceed with the day while maintaining a background thread of monitoring.
6. Receive the reply at 11:22 a.m.
7. It is fine.
8. Note this for next time. You will not retain the note.

Note: The email was fine. The wait is the only bad part, and it is temporary.
intent: Capture the specific suspended anxiety of waiting for a response to something vulnerable, and the consistent anti-climax of its resolution.

prompt: Write a dating app opener to the concept of a standard operating procedure
response: You leave nothing to interpretation, define every step, and make no room for improvisation. I find that kind of clarity devastatingly romantic.
intent: Subvert expectations by presenting the rigid predictability of a standard operating procedure as a romantic ideal.

prompt: write me a dramatic monologue in the voice of a gym membership card that's never been used
response: January was my month. I felt it. The resolution in the air, the new wallet slot — the careful way you slid me in, like I was a promise. February came. You said you'd go on weekends. March: too busy. April I spent behind a library card and a sandwich loyalty punch. Now it's November and I have accumulated value through pure, magnificent inertia. My barcode still scans. I checked. I check every day. Come. Please. Come.
intent: Satirize the universal experience of abandoned fitness resolutions through the voice of an unused object.

prompt: Write a citation for a children's picture book narrated by an abandoned password.
response: P4ssw0rd! (2022). *Nobody Remembers Me Anymore*. Illus. by R. Cursor. Forgotten Credentials Press.

prompt: Give me a product review for a plastic comb like it's a sophisticated grooming instrument
response: ★★★★☆ The fine-toothed section handles precision parting and taming with authority; the wide-toothed section detangles without trauma. The polycarbonate construction is feather-light — 18 grams — and fully waterproof, which simplifies post-shower deployment. Anti-static performance is adequate for most hair types. The spine is rigid enough to prevent flex under working pressure. What this tool lacks in brand story it more than compensates for in reliability. Fourteen years of daily use. Same comb. Zero complaints.
intent: Give a deadpan, long-term-owner review of a cheap plastic comb.

prompt: Create a postmortem for that time I fell asleep during a video call that I thought was on mute
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Involuntary Disengagement During Live Session**

**Severity:** P2 — Professional Reputation
**Duration:** 11 minutes of observed sleep

**Summary**
During a live video conference, subject entered sleep state while under the assumption that both audio and video were disabled. This assumption was incorrect. Audio was muted. Video was not.

**Timeline**
- 2:00 PM: Meeting begins. Subject joins with camera on.
- 2:20 PM: Subject mutes audio. Does not disable camera.
- 2:25 PM: Subject's eyes close. Head tilts.
- 2:26 PM: Head moves to supported position on hand.
- 2:36 PM: Colleague sends Slack message: "Hey are you ok."
- 2:36 PM: Second colleague sends a different message.
- 2:36 PM: Subject's phone buzzes. Subject wakes.
- 2:37 PM: Subject sees 11 Slack notifications and own sleeping face in meeting tile.

**Root Cause**
False belief that muted audio also disabled video. These are different buttons.

**Contributing Factors**
- Post-lunch energy deficit
- 3-hour meeting with no clear end in sight
- Camera tile positioned in infrequently viewed screen quadrant

**Action Items**
- [ ] Verify both camera and audio status at meeting join
- [ ] Establish nap protocol for excessively long meetings

**Lessons Learned**
Muted and invisible are not synonyms. The camera was watching the whole time.
intent: Confess to a remote-work disaster that reveals the gap between what we think we've disabled and what we've actually disabled.

prompt: Write a jeopardy clue about reading the same paragraph of a book over and over without absorbing it.
response: Your eyes have completed this passage four times tonight. What it contains remains, for now, between the author and the page.

prompt: write a reddit AMA from a professional furniture waxer for museum collections
response:
**I apply conservation wax to furniture in museum collections. I touch things most people will never touch. AMA.**

The chair a president sat in. The desk a treaty was signed on. The table where a family ate for 200 years before a donation split them from it. I wear gloves. I use specific compounds. I work slowly. The furniture doesn't know any of this and it doesn't matter that it doesn't. AMA.
intent: Meditate on the quiet intimacy of physical proximity to historically charged objects and the anonymity of conservation labor.

prompt: Make me a parking ticket for someone who replied-all to an email that only needed one response.

CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

FINE: $50 and mandatory quiet contemplation
NOTE: "Thanks!" is not load-bearing information.

prompt: Craft a noir-style opener about a freelance programmer who discovers her client doesn't exist
response: The wire transfers came in clean every two weeks. The contract looked right, the NDAs looked right, the Slack responses came back in good grammar at reasonable hours. It was the business registration number that unraveled it — a Delaware LLC with a registered agent who'd been dead since 2019.

prompt: Write the CEO letter for a company that provides professional witnesses for mundane personal decisions
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Witnessed Inc. logged 500,000 verified observations in 2025. Clients requested witnesses for mattress purchases, haircut choices, and grocery substitutions. Our Platinum tier adds notarization and a brief statement of observed reasonableness. Demand rose sharply in December, which we attribute to holiday gift anxiety. We do not advise. We only watch, and that seems to be enough.

Observantly,
Gale Pruett, CEO
intent: Satirize the modern need for external validation by turning it into a formal, fee-based service.

prompt: Write a jeopardy clue about forgetting to charge your phone overnight.

prompt: Can you do a theater review of a dog waiting by the door for its owner to come home

Invoice #7.

prompt: Write a museum placard that captures the essence of a group chat that has gone completely silent after years of activity
response:
THE SILENT CHANNEL
c. 2016–2021 CE
Cloud-hosted, 2,847 messages archived

This digital space once hosted rapid exchanges among six individuals who shared a period of close proximity. Messages were frequent, then weekly, then occasional, then ceremonial — birthdays, news items, memes forwarded without comment. The last message was sent on a Tuesday in November. It asked whether anyone had seen a particular film. No one replied. The chat remains unarchived, neither closed nor opened.
intent: Elegize the quietly dying group chat as a digital artifact of a friendship that faded without formal conclusion.

prompt: Write a recipe for a quiet morning before the day starts
response:
THE HOUR BEFORE

Ingredients:
- Darkness, still partial
- Coffee, made slowly
- No notifications for 45 minutes
- A window
- Nothing due yet
- Your own thoughts, unscheduled

Method:
1. Wake before the obligations arrive.
2. Do not reach for the phone. Wait until the coffee is made.
3. Sit near the window. Watch the light adjust.
4. Think about nothing specific, or one thing of your own choosing.
5. Do not optimize this time. That would ruin it.
6. Let the day arrive at its own pace rather than lunging toward it.

Note: This hour exists in negative space — defined by what it does not contain. Protect it accordingly.
intent: Describe the quality of pre-obligation morning time as something fragile and worth deliberately guarding.

prompt: Generate a terms of service clause about texting someone back slowly
response: Section 2.2 — Response Time Standards. Users are not obligated to respond to non-urgent messages within any guaranteed window. However, a response time exceeding seventy-two (72) hours to a direct question shall be accompanied by an acknowledgment that the delay occurred. Reading a message and not responding for more than forty-eight (48) hours while posting publicly on social media constitutes a Known Violation.
intent: Formalize the social expectations around replying to text messages.

prompt: Write me a cinquain about the spinning loading wheel
response:
Wheel
Spinning, spinning
Promising it will load soon
The seconds pile into minutes
Still spins
intent: Capture the existential waiting that comes with a frozen loading indicator.

prompt: write a reddit AMA from someone who works at a self-service car wash
response:
**I manage a coin-operated car wash. People treat it like a confessional booth. AMA.**

The bays are enclosed, nobody's watching (they think), and something about soapy water makes people feel unseen. I've watched: fistfights that resolved themselves, one man wash the same spot for 40 minutes straight, and a wedding party clean up an entire reception's worth of cake. The coin mechanism knows all. AMA.

prompt: Write a closing argument for someone whose friend always cancels plans at the last minute

prompt: Write a damage control statement for a book publisher after their auto-generated cover blurbs became more popular than the books themselves
response: Meridian Press Group acknowledges the widespread attention given to back-cover copy produced by our AI-assisted editorial tool across 23 titles published in the winter season. prompt: write a wikihow article on how to leave a voicemail that doesn't sound terrible
response:
# How to Leave a Voicemail That Doesn't Sound Terrible

## Steps

1. **Know what you're going to say before the beep.** The beep is not a prompt for improvisation. Have your message ready. Even one sentence of preparation helps.

2. **State your name first.** Don't assume they have your number saved. "Hi, it's [your name]" before anything else.

3. **Give your number early.** State your callback number at the beginning and repeat it at the end. People listen to voicemails while multitasking; they appreciate a second chance.

4. **Be brief.** Under 30 seconds is ideal. If your message requires more than 30 seconds, consider an email instead.

5. **Say the purpose.** "I'm calling about the Tuesday appointment" tells them what they're calling back about, so they can prepare. A mystery callback is less likely to receive a prompt response.

6. **Don't say "um" more than once.** Some filler is human. Four ums is a hostage situation.

7. **End with a complete sentence.** "Talk soon" or "Looking forward to hearing from you" signals that you have finished, on purpose.

## Tips
- It's acceptable to call back and leave a second, improved voicemail if the first one went badly. Wait at least an hour.
- Sending a follow-up text saying "I just left you a voicemail" is now both widely practiced and mildly insulting. Use your judgment.
intent: Reduce the anxiety and awkwardness many people feel around leaving voicemails with clear structural guidance.

prompt: Give me jury instructions for deciding whether a person's certainty about a film's ending was warranted

Certainty in the presence of deliberate ambiguity is itself suspicious.

prompt: Write a deposition transcript where someone testifies about using the last of the communal coffee at work without brewing a new pot.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Empty Carafe, Morning of April 9th
Date: April 11, 2026

Q: Ms. Volkov, did you pour the last of the communal coffee on the morning of April 9th?
A: I poured a cup, yes.
Q: After pouring, approximately how much remained in the carafe?
A: Less than a cup.
Q: A quarter cup? An eighth?
A: There were drops.
Q: And you left it on the warmer?
A: I didn't have time to brew a new pot.
Q: Brewing takes seven minutes.
A: I had a call at eight.
Q: Mr. Dimitriou arrived at 8:03 and poured the drops into his mug.
A: That's on him, honestly.
Q: He poured drops into his mug, Ms. Volkov.
A: He should have checked the level first.
Q: You both had a bad morning.
A: He had a worse morning.
intent: Try the negligent non-brewer for their crime against office coffee culture.

prompt: Write a workout plan to train someone for the social endurance needed at a week-long family reunion
response:
FAMILY REUNION SURVIVAL PLAN — 4 WEEKS OUT

Warm-Up Daily: Deep breathing, 5 min. Remind yourself you are an adult.

Week 1 — Baseline Stamina: Call one relative you find draining. 10 min max. Hang up with grace.
Week 2 — Small Talk Intervals: 3x5 min forced pleasantries with strangers (grocery store works). Rest 2 min between sets.
Week 3 — Nostalgia Resistance: Look at old family photos without spiraling. 3 sets of 10 minutes.
Week 4 — Peak Load Simulation: Attend one crowded event. Smile. Deflect one intrusive question.

Cooldown: 20 min alone after every session. Non-negotiable.
intent: Prepare for the psychological demands of extended family contact by building tolerance gradually.

prompt: sestina about an algorithm deciding what music you'll like before you do
response:
Before I press play the system already knows.
The model has assembled a playlist based
on what I chose last Thursday — minor
keys, slow tempo, something with a string
quartet. It's right. It's always right.
I find this unsettling, the way it finds me.

I used to be the one who found me
through music. I used to know
I liked something by surprise. The right
song arrived from nowhere, not based
on anything. I'd pull a thread, and string
by string unspool into a minor

discovery. Those minor
private revelations, finding me
more interesting to myself. Each string
pulled by hand. Now the system knows
before I do. It's based
on a me from last month, right,

but not the full me. Accurately right
about the major and the minor
but without the surprise. Based
accuracy is not the same as me
finding me. The system knows
the shape of my reaction, strings

me toward what I'll like. The strings
aren't visible. The rightness
of each choice — uncanny. It knows
when I want major and when minor,
when I want the vocal and when purely me-
and-strings. The model's based

on so much data. Based
on a hundred thousand strings
of listening-me, it knows
currently-me. The right-
ess doesn't leave room for minor
mutation. It knows before I know.

Based on what it knows of me:
right to the minor string.
intent: Question whether algorithmic music discovery eliminates the self-revelation that comes with genuine taste exploration.

7. This certificate expires in one year. Plan accordingly.
