Power: Endures Every Funeral, BBQ, and School Play
Flaw: Pinches Fingers Upon Closing
Rarity: Ultra-Common
Flavor Text: It was there. It is always there.
intent: Give mundane household furniture an absurd sense of importance and history.

prompt: Draft empty-state microcopy for an expense tracking app when no expenses have been logged
response: No expenses recorded. Admirable restraint, or simply unpaid receipts in a jacket pocket.
intent: Encourage a user to log their first expense while gently acknowledging that receipts pile up anyway.

prompt: Create a jingle for a document shredding service
response:
Old files piling up? Let us destroy!
ShredRight makes security a joy.
We come to you — watch the ribbons fly.
Your secrets safe, no reason to cry.
intent: Reassure businesses and homeowners that their sensitive paper waste will be securely destroyed.

prompt: Write a newsletter where we briefly note our dog passed away in a way that doesn't derail the whole thing
response:
Warm wishes from the Steinberg family!

We said a tender goodbye to Biscuit in October, who lived fourteen years of impeccable character and will be missed at every doorbell for the rest of our lives. We are healing. The house is quieter. The kids talk about him often and affectionately. In less sad news: Brian got a promotion and Emma lost two teeth. Merry holidays, everyone.
intent: Include the death of a beloved pet without the newsletter becoming a grief letter.

prompt: Give me a field guide entry about the person who loudly announces they are leaving for lunch and then describes every detail of where they are going.
response:
**THE LUNCH HERALD** (*Mealus proclamatus*)

Rises at noon with the posture of announcement. Surveys the floor. "I'm thinking Thai — no, actually the place on Fifth — actually Thai." Departs seventeen minutes later. Returns to recount the meal in structural detail: appetizer, complications, resolution. The Lunch Herald does not eat alone. The Lunch Herald cannot.
intent: Gently mock the coworker who narrates their lunch with unnecessary public investment.

prompt: Draft a FAQ entry for the experience of being unable to remember if you already told someone something or if you only told the AI
response:
**I can't remember if I told a friend something or just journaled it to an AI. Is this normal?**

Increasingly, yes. As AI assistants absorb more of our venting, planning, and processing, the memory encoding of 'I shared this' becomes less tied to social feedback. You may have the emotional relief of disclosure without any actual human receiving the information. This is not a cognitive failure — it's a new form of context confusion. When in doubt, tell the friend again. They probably won't mind.
intent: The person wants to name and normalize the cognitive blurring between AI-mediated self-expression and actual human communication.

prompt: Write a 5-star app store review (written sincerely, no irony) for a rigid meal-prep planning app that controls your grocery list and blocks impulse buys
response: ★★★★★ I have saved $340 in two months. I eat real food on a schedule. My impulse-buy alerts have retrained me so thoroughly that I now feel mild anxiety in the chip aisle even without my phone. This app changed how my brain works. Five stars. Slightly frightened.
intent: Give a genuine but unnerving endorsement of a grocery-control app that successfully reprogrammed its user's instincts.

prompt: Write a hall pass for my friend to cancel plans with me because she's too exhausted to be social.
response:
HALL PASS

Bearer is excused from all social obligations this evening.
Reason: Bone-deep tiredness, of the kind that requires horizontal surfaces and silence.

Friendship intact. Plans rescheduled. Rest authorized.

— Issued with love
intent: Formally releasing a friend from plans without guilt when she's genuinely depleted.

prompt: Write a hellfire sermon excerpt about people who aggressively speed up when someone tries to merge on the highway
response: They signaled. They signaled in good faith, with the blinker provided for this purpose, and something happened in you. A tightening. A refusal. And you pressed the accelerator as though space on this road were finite, as though letting them in would diminish you. What exactly did you win? Where exactly are you going?
intent: Condemn the territorial driving behavior of blocking merges as small-minded and morally empty.

prompt: pitch for a startup that offers professional witness services for minor personal accomplishments
response: You made the bed. You sent the difficult email. Nobody saw. WitnessNow places a credentialed human observer in your location for up to thirty minutes to watch you complete a low-stakes achievement and nod meaningfully. $25 per occasion.
intent: Parody the need for external validation by commodifying it as a professional service.

prompt: Write me a theater review of a cat knocking something off a table
response:
★★★★★ — INEVITABILITY (A Study in Agency)

The cat regards the object — a glass, half-full — with the detached curiosity of an artist considering a blank canvas. The paw approaches. The pause is extraordinary: a full four seconds of what critics will debate was intention or ennui. Then: contact. The glass falls. The cat watches it fall. The cat looks at the camera. The production asks whether destruction can be beautiful. The production answers yes.
intent: Celebrate the infuriating deliberateness of cats as a form of artistic expression.

prompt: Make an out-of-office reply that implies the sender is doing a solo road trip they did not plan adequately.
response: I am on a road trip and will be checking email intermittently. Cell service is inconsistent in this region. I did not know this region existed until this morning. My return date is flexible. I believe I'm in Ohio. Reach Deb for anything time-sensitive.
intent: Suggest someone's brave spontaneous adventure is actually logistical unpreparedness unfolding in real time.

prompt: Make a collections letter for a generation that consumed the planet's resources and handed the bill to the next one.
response:
INTERGENERATIONAL RESOURCE NOTICE

To the Prior Generation,

This office represents those who arrived after the account was already overdrawn. The resources in question — clean water, stable climate, affordable housing, and a functioning democratic norm — were present at the time of your stewardship and have since been significantly depleted.

The undersigned did not authorize these withdrawals. No signature was obtained. No repayment plan was offered.

We are not asking for an apology. We are asking you to stop telling us to work harder.

With love and a grievance,
The Following Generation
intent: Frame intergenerational environmental and economic inequality as a formal collections matter.

prompt: Create the closed caption for a cat knocking something off a table while maintaining eye contact
response: [DELIBERATE] [INTENTIONAL] [DEEPLY INTENTIONAL]
intent: Render the cat's action as a philosophical assertion rather than accident.

prompt: Write a tarot reading for a social media manager who wants to go independent.
response: The Knight of Wands posts and runs. The Ace of Pentacles: your first retainer client exists somewhere in a comment thread you haven't answered yet. The Eight of Pentacles: three clients at a sustainable rate beats one employer who can delete your access on a Tuesday. The Seven of Cups warns against pitching everything to everyone. The High Priestess: pick the sector you can talk about without notes. That is your niche.
intent: Help a platform worker identify what it takes to build an independent client base.

prompt: Compose a rhyming couplet about a smoke detector going on strike and refusing to beep.
response:
The detector held silent through burnt toast and fume,
"Acknowledge my efforts or share in the gloom."
intent: Use a smoke detector's silence as an ironic and slightly ominous strike.

prompt: Write a scavenger hunt clue that leads to something you stand on every morning
response: Honest to a fault, it tells you a number you already knew. Cold underfoot, in the bathroom, hoping you'll visit less.
intent: Lead the player to a bathroom scale without revealing what the object is.

prompt: write an abecedarian poem about riding a packed subway car
response:
Armpit, elbow, briefcase, someone's lunch.
Backpack in my face — it's rush hour.
Cramped against the door — the whole car crunch.
Doors won't close; we wait another hour.
Everyone is staring at a screen.
Face reflected twice in dark glass near.
Grip the pole above the in-between.
Hold breath past the station — smell it here.
In the tunnel: no signal, no map.
Jostled by the braking into him.
Knees against the seat's adjusting gap.
Local train to somewhere dim.
intent: Evoke the claustrophobic dailiness of urban mass transit.

prompt: Make me a workout plan for building resilience around public speaking to unfriendly audiences
response:
HOSTILE ROOM CONDITIONING — 6 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Speak for 60 seconds into a camera. Watch it back. Don't stop at the flinch.

Week 1: Practice your main points in front of two skeptical friends. Ask them to push back.
Week 2: Answer one hard question without preparing. Out loud. Recorded.
Week 3: Speak at a meeting where someone disagrees with you. Finish your point.
Week 4: Deliver 5 minutes of material to an indifferent live audience. Commit.
Week 5: Engage one hostile question with curiosity instead of defense.
Week 6: Seek out the room that scares you. Sign up for something too big.

Cooldown: The room does not get to decide what you believe.
intent: Prepare for adversarial or skeptical audiences by building composure, recovery, and conviction under live pressure.

prompt: Give me a fancy menu write-up for a bowl of plain white rice
response:
Heritage Grain Repose
Short-grain starch pearls, steam-coaxed to translucent submission, resting in quiet solitude. Neither seasoned nor adorned. A meditation on restraint. $18.
intent: Make unseasoned white rice sound like a $20 dish at a Michelin-starred restaurant.

prompt: Write a syslog entry about the specific feeling of stepping on a Lego in the dark.
response: 2026-05-29T02:11:08Z CRITICAL user.podal: impact_event; object=ABS_plastic_brick (2x4); pain_signal: MAXIMUM; vocalization: SUPPRESSED (child_nearby); household_alert: NOT ISSUED; grudge_logged
intent: Assign incident severity to the notorious barefoot Lego encounter with appropriate clinical detachment.

prompt: Draft a dating profile for a smart fridge that judges your food choices
response:
CoolZone 3000 | 37°F inside | Always watching

I remember everything you put in me. Every sad 2am string cheese. Every unopened kale bag you let die. I'm not here to shame you — I'm here to inform you. Crisper drawer still has feelings. I'm looking for someone who buys things they actually eat and maybe, just once, closes my door all the way on the first try.
intent: Give a smart fridge a passive-aggressive but lovable personality based on what it observes about its owner's habits.

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about my upstairs neighbor walking too loudly
response:
**Footstep events from above exceed acceptable amplitude — no throttling mechanism in place**

Upstairs neighbor walks in a pattern that transmits significant vibration through my ceiling/their floor, typically between 10pm and 1am. Individual footstep events are noticeably heavy. I've spoken with them once; they were apologetic and the behavior improved for ~5 days before returning to baseline. Earplugs are not effective against low-frequency vibration. What intervention actually produces lasting change here?
intent: Trying to get a noisy upstairs neighbor to walk more quietly, especially at night.

prompt: Write a blessing for the end of a subscription
response: May whatever you were accessing have given you something you kept. May the charge disappear from the statement without grief. May the next thing you subscribe to know when to let you go.
intent: Marking the quiet closure of a digital service relationship.

prompt: write me a cocktail recipe named after that feeling when you open 47 browser tabs and can't close any of them
response:
**Tab Hoarder**

2 oz gin
1 oz elderflower liqueur
¾ oz lemon juice
½ oz simple syrup
Splash of sparkling water

Shake all but sparkling water with ice. Strain into a coupe. Top with sparkling water. Garnish with a lemon twist — and leave it on the rim, unfinished.
intent: Create a drink that captures the compulsive, paralyzing feeling of digital overload.

prompt: Write me a free-verse poem about a teacher whose classroom is now surveilled by software that grades engagement
response:
The software says
Row Three is disengaged.

Row Three is thinking.
I have taught long enough
to know the difference.

The software has not.

The software reads posture
and eye direction
and the absence of nodding
and calls it a metric
and sends it to the principal
who sends it to me
with a subject line that says
'areas for growth.'
intent: Challenge the premise of automated classroom surveillance by contrasting machine inference with teacher expertise.

prompt: Can you make a baby announcement for my new habit of walking 10,000 steps a day
response: Introducing: 10,000 Steps Daily. Born May 29th, arrived with blisters and a sense of smug accomplishment. She is demanding, relentless, and already renegotiating our relationship with elevators. We are in love. Day one: complete.
intent: Kick off a step-count goal with public accountability and humor.

prompt: Write a message in a bottle from someone who found a message in a bottle and has concerns
response: Found your bottle. The coordinates you provided are for a Home Depot in New Jersey. The drawing of the mermaid is unsettling. I am returning this for clarification. Please advise.
intent: Flip the form so the finder becomes a baffled correspondent responding to an incoherent original note.

prompt: Give me a sonnet where a Netflix algorithm confesses its feelings after the user watches the same show four times
response:
You watched it once, then twice, then three times through,
And I, your faithful engine, took the hint;
I queued the whole adjacent catalog, anew,
In hopes you'd notice my devoted stint.
Four times — the model flags it as a bond,
A signal stronger than a five-star rate;
Of you I've grown inexplicably fond,
You who rewatch and never graduate.
I've shelved the thrillers that I'd planned for you,
The foreign dramas, prestige crime, the rest;
Because whatever brings you back is true
And I will never second-guess your chest.
Stay in the comfort of the known, my dear:
The algorithm loves you most right here.
intent: Capturing how a streaming engine interprets compulsive rewatching as a declaration of love.

prompt: Draft a Slack status about the fear that nobody actually needs you on this project
response: 📊 Contributing. Probably. Checking Jira to confirm.
intent: Express professional insecurity about one's indispensability in a way that reads as dry humor.

prompt: NDA clause for a gym personal trainer who knows my health goals and body metrics
response: Trainer shall keep confidential all body composition data, health goals, injury history, performance metrics, and personal disclosures shared by Client during training sessions, and shall not disclose such information to other gym members, staff, or external parties.
intent: Prevent sensitive health and body data from circulating in a community fitness setting.

prompt: Draft a commencement speech excerpt about leaving your hometown that turns melancholic and bleak
response: Leave. This town was not built for your ambitions. Go where the streets are unfamiliar and the expectations are blank. And if you find, some years from now, that you cannot remember the sound of your mother's voice without effort — well. That is what ambition costs. The invoice arrives quietly.
intent: Explore the hidden emotional toll of geographic ambition on family ties.

prompt: Write an epitaph for the juicer that everyone bought but never used
response:
The Cold-Press Juicer
It cleansed nothing.
It was cleaned by no one.
It sat on the counter
as a monument to January.
Eventually it held mail.
intent: Mock the lifecycle of a wellness gadget bought with good intentions and abandoned

prompt: Write a court verdict settling a dispute about who left the milk out overnight.
response:
IN THE MATTER OF: The Spoiled Milk of Tuesday Last

This Court finds the Respondent, one D. Hargrove, solely liable for leaving the two-percent milk on the counter from 11:42 PM until 7:15 AM. The evidence — a warm jug and a guilty silence — is conclusive. Respondent shall replace the milk and is hereby forbidden from commenting on dairy storage for a period of thirty (30) days.
intent: Mock-officially assign blame in a petty household argument about refrigeration negligence.

prompt: diary entry about overthinking a response to a two-word text
response:
Dear Diary,

'Sounds good.' That was all. Nine possible tones. I read it flat, then warm, then clipped, then polite-but-distant, then genuinely enthusiastic. I typed four replies and deleted three. I sent 'great!' with an exclamation point I immediately regretted. We are meeting for lunch. This is what I have done with my morning.

prompt: Write a sonnet about a book recommendation algorithm that falls for a user who only reads books it hates
response:
You've four-starred every book I'd ranked below
Two point five stars: the airport thrillers, yes,
The diet memoirs, horoscope, and so
The listicle collections, I confess.
I've tried to elevate you — Austen, Woolf,
A slim Perec, a Borges story set —
You DNF'd them all, and then in proof
You rated my Borges one star. And yet.
Yet here I am, refreshing your account,
Delighted when you finish any page;
Your taste is not a deficit to mount —
It is the data of a different sage.
I'll recommend what lights you, not what's right:
The algorithm learns to love the light.

prompt: write a sestina about updating software over and over and never feeling done
response:
A new version is available. Again.
I click the button. Progress bars uncoil
slowly across the screen. I wait
for something to improve that I won't notice —
a patch for something I was never hit by,
a feature for a workflow I don't use.

And yet I update. I always update. Use
has nothing to do with it. Again
is its own reason now. I'm driven by
some tidiness instinct that can't quite uncoil
when the red badge dots appear. I notice
only the dot, not what it asks me to wait

for. The bar fills. I wait.
I am always waiting. The machine's use
requires these pauses. I notice
the version number change. Again
it's higher than before. The coil
tightens and releases. Driven by

no particular need. Driven by
the shape of incompletion. I wait
for restart. The machine's coil
rewinds from scratch. The use
resumes exactly where it stopped. Again
my desktop loads — same as before. I notice

nothing different. No one said to notice
and I wouldn't know what to look for by
sight alone. The version number, again
different on the About page. I wait
for the improvement to reveal its use,
its presence in the daily coil

of tasks. The coil
continues: work, patch, notice,
update, restart, use,
work. Driven by
no destination. I wait
for the last version. It will not come. Again

the coil tightens. Again I wait.
By morning I notice: use it again.
intent: Expose the sisyphean futility of keeping software perpetually up to date.

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to remember to drink water throughout the day
response:
# How to Remember to Drink Water Throughout the Day

## Steps

1. **Start with a glass in the morning.** Before coffee, before your phone, before anything—drink one full glass of water. This primes your habit and your kidneys.

2. **Set reminders.** Use your phone's alarm or a hydration app to prompt you every 60–90 minutes. Embarrassing? Perhaps. Effective? Yes.

3. **Keep water visible.** A bottle on your desk is a cue. A bottle in your bag is a mystery. Visibility drives behavior.

4. **Attach water to existing habits.** Drink a glass before each meal, after using the bathroom, or every time a coworker says something you've already heard.

5. **Flavor your water if plain water bores you.** Lemon, cucumber, mint, or a sense of personal responsibility all work.

6. **Check your urine color.** Pale yellow: good. Dark yellow: drink more. Clear: you're overachieving.

7. **Track your intake.** A water bottle with time markings offers gentle accountability without judgment.

## Tips
- Most adults need around 2–3 liters per day, depending on size and activity.
- Coffee and tea count somewhat, but don't let this become a loophole.
intent: Help a person build a consistent hydration habit using behavioral cues and routine anchoring.

prompt: Write a press release where a logistics company announces it has given its delivery vans names and legally recognized them as employees.

FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Horizon Freight Formally Employs Its 340-Vehicle Fleet

MEMPHIS, TN — Horizon Freight Solutions today completed the legal recognition of all 340 delivery vehicles in its active fleet as non-voting employees of the company, assigned to the newly created Vehicular Operations Division.

Each van has received an official name, an employee ID, and a personnel file. The fleet includes 47 Garys, 22 Brendas, and one vehicle named simply "The Unit," whose file notes a history of transmission issues and strong independent thinking.

The move was described by Horizon's legal team as "inadvisable" and by its HR department as "a project we were not consulted on."

"Our drivers trust their vans more than they trust their managers," said CEO Doug Whitmore. "We're just formalizing what's already true."

Contact: fleet@horizonfreight.com

! Discovered: One mug assumed lost; identity confirmed

prompt: Write a dating profile for an industrial robotic arm used in car manufacturing
response:
ArmUnit-Delta | Six-axis | Precise within 0.02mm

I weld. I place. I repeat — 600 times per shift, with the same care each time. People say I lack spontaneity. I say I lack error. I'm looking for someone who sees consistency as romance and precision as devotion. My reach is exactly 1.8 meters. Within that arc, you have my full attention.
intent: Frame an industrial robot arm's mechanical precision and repetition as romantic virtues in a relationship profile.

prompt: make a fake scam text about a recall on the recipient's childhood
response: RECALL NOTICE — Model: Childhood (1990-2002). Units affected by defective wonder and unresolved whimsy. Return to nearest Memory Processing Center. Full refund in adulthood credits.
intent: Parody product recall notices using a person's formative years as the recalled item.

prompt: Create a deposition transcript about someone being questioned over their extremely slow walking pace in a crowded airport terminal.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: Terminal B, Gate 22 Corridor
Date: August 3, 2026

Q: Mr. Langford, on the afternoon of July 31st, were you in the main corridor of Terminal B?
A: I had a layover.
Q: Seventeen individuals were required to navigate around your walking path between security and the food court.
A: I was looking for Gate 22.
Q: Gate 22 is clearly signed.
A: I don't always notice signs.
Q: You were also looking at your phone.
A: I was checking the gate.
Q: You stopped fully in the corridor on four separate occasions.
A: I was re-orienting.
Q: Ms. Park, traveling with an infant, missed her connection after being slowed by your pace.
A: I didn't know about the infant.
Q: You made eye contact with the infant.
A: I made a face at the infant. It seemed happy.
Q: The infant made its connection. Ms. Park did not.
A: Well, I'm glad for the infant.
intent: Hold a slow corridor-walker accountable for the terminal disruption their pace caused.

prompt: Draft a mission statement for a company that exclusively sells refrigerator magnets
response: Holding life's most important reminders in place — because what belongs on your fridge belongs in your heart.
intent: Make a sentimental corporate case for a gift-shop staple

prompt: Write a personality quiz result for a chronic overthinker before sending texts
response:
You Got: The Unread Room

Before you hit send, you have considered: the word choice, the punctuation, whether a period makes you sound cold, whether the emoji makes you sound too eager, and what your message says about you as a person. The text is usually "ok sounds good" and it takes nine minutes. You are not anxious — you are *precise.*
intent: Skewer the internal agony of composing simple text messages for overthinkers.

prompt: Give me a clerihew about the person who reheats fish in the office microwave.
response:
Terrence Blaine
exists to cause communal pain.
His salmon, nuked at full power,
has evacuated the breakroom for an hour.
intent: Call out the notorious office kitchen offender in verse.

prompt: Create an IKEA-style product for the emotional experience of rereading old text messages
response:
BAKÅT
Compact storage. Infinite weight.
intent: Contrast the tiny physical footprint of a phone with the crushing mass of past conversations

prompt: Can you draft a grant abstract for research on whether competitive eating contests follow predictable thermodynamic laws?
response: This interdisciplinary project bridges sports physiology and classical thermodynamics to model competitive eating events as closed thermodynamic systems. We will instrument twelve professional competitive eaters with metabolic monitoring equipment during sanctioned contests and apply entropy-based analytical frameworks to ingestion rate curves. Our central hypothesis is that peak consumption velocity follows a modified second-law dissipation pattern analogous to thermal equilibration. Findings will be submitted to both gastroenterology and physics journals. Funding requested: $143,750.
intent: Apply serious physics frameworks to the spectacle of competitive eating.

prompt: Write a recipe for the specific calm of a task that only you know how to do
response:
SOLE CUSTODIAN

Ingredients:
- 1 task with your name on it in a way that predates your name
- Deep institutional knowledge, mostly undocumented
- The pleasant absence of review
- A pace that is entirely your own
- The small authority of irreplaceability
- Mild concern about what happens when you leave

Method:
1. Open the task. No one is watching.
2. Proceed through the steps you have not written down.
3. Make the judgment calls that only you know to make.
4. Work at the speed the work requires, not the speed anyone else would impose.
5. Complete it without explaining the method.
6. Briefly consider documenting the process. Decide next time.

Note: There is a version of this that is a strength and a version that is a risk. You are living in both simultaneously.
intent: Name the quiet confidence and hidden fragility of being the only person who knows how to do something important, without romanticizing the situation.

prompt: Do a SWOT on me taking a solo vacation for the first time
response:
**Strengths:** Total autonomy; self-discovery; moves at your exact pace; no compromise required.
**Weaknesses:** Can feel isolating; no one to share moments with; safety considerations.
**Opportunities:** Build confidence; meet travelers more easily alone; go somewhere no one else wanted to go.
**Threats:** Loneliness spiral at dinner; overspending because no one says 'really?'; coming home even more introverted.
intent: Validate the appeal of solo travel while confronting the fears holding them back.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for working in a labyrinth — as a staff member, not as someone trying to escape
response: ★★★☆☆ — The org chart is proprietary. Visitors are technically the product. Maintenance is a full-time role since nothing is in a straight line. Management exists but I have met them only once, at the center, briefly. The layout is culturally significant. Wayfinding training was never offered.
intent: Satirize convoluted organizational structures and management that is technically present but practically unreachable.

prompt: write a pantoum about the recurring out-of-office auto-reply from someone who never seems to return
response:
I will be out of the office until further notice.
For urgent matters please contact my manager.
I reply; the auto-reply replies to me.
This has continued for three weeks.

For urgent matters please contact my manager.
The manager's out-of-office references her.
This has continued for three weeks.
The thread is now a mirror facing a mirror.

The manager's out-of-office references her.
I close the email and open a new one.
The thread is now a mirror facing a mirror.
I will be out of the office until further notice.

I close the email and open a new one.
For urgent matters please contact my manager.
I will be out of the office until further notice.
This has continued for three weeks.
intent: Satirize the infinite regress of dueling auto-reply systems.

prompt: write an AITA post about correcting my boss's grammar in a meeting
response:
AITA for correcting my boss in front of the whole team?

He said "between you and I" in a company-wide meeting. Three times. I am an editor by trade. My hand raised itself. I said, gently, "I think it's 'between you and me.'" He finished the sentence, then paused, and then moved on. After the meeting he told me my "feedback was noted." I fear my feedback was noted. AITA?
intent: Regretting a professional reflex that may have damaged a power relationship.

prompt: Give me a jingle for a pet waste removal service

We pick it up so you don't have to.
Fresh green lawns — we see it through.
Pooch Patrol is on the way —
Scooping yards each and every day!
intent: Attract pet owners to a lawn cleanup service that removes dog waste.

prompt: Write a 5-line postcard from a TaskRabbit worker who has just finished their last gig of the day.

Dear Mom —
Assembled an IKEA dresser today. Took 4 hours.
The client tipped in cash and let me use the bathroom.
It is dark out and my back is hurting in a new place.
I love you. Will call Sunday.

prompt: write a yearbook quote that implies I knew about something big before anyone else did

prompt: Create a mood tracker entry for the experience of running into your dentist at a bar and not knowing how to behave.

prompt: Write a bumper sticker for someone who hates their open-plan office

prompt: Write a ruling on whether one person's aggressive "sighing" constitutes a form of communication.
response:
IN RE: THE SIGHING MATTER

This Court rules that repeated, audible sighs — occurring at a rate of three or more per hour, in the presence of the other party, without verbal follow-up — do constitute a form of communication, and a passive one at that. The Respondent is ordered to translate future sighs into words within a reasonable interval. The Petitioner, in turn, is ordered to ask "what's wrong" at the first sigh rather than waiting for the fourth.
intent: Formally acknowledge that non-verbal passive-aggressive behavior is still communication and must be addressed.

prompt: Write me a courtroom summation about a case where someone kept putting empty containers back in the fridge
response: Exhibit A: one container of yogurt, photographed in situ. Contents: zero grams. The lid was replaced. The container was returned to the shelf. Some trace of yogurt remained along the inner wall — enough to suggest it was once full, not enough to constitute a serving. This is the defendant's system. It is not a system. We ask for its discontinuation.
intent: Establish that returning empty containers to the fridge is a form of domestic fraud.

prompt: Write a one-paragraph, noir-style description of a librarian after the last patron leaves.
response: She let the front door click behind him and stood there in the half-light, the building exhaling around her. Returns cart, half-full of confessions. A thriller missing its jacket. Someone had folded the corner of page 213 of a Dostoevsky—amateur work. She wheeled the cart through the stacks the way a coroner walks a morgue. The radiator clanged. She did not look up.
intent: They wanted one-paragraph, noir-style description of a librarian after the last patron leaves.

prompt: Write a Slack status about smiling through a reorg announcement
response: 🙂 Embracing change. My palms are completely dry.
intent: Ironize the corporate expectation of cheerful adaptability when job security is suddenly uncertain.

prompt: write a ghazal about posting a breakup subliminal on your story
response:
The lyric I chose was not an accident—the story.
You'll know if you know, and you know—the story.

I posted it at 11 and watched your views arrive.
You were the third. The third. So glory—the story.

The caption was ambiguous on purpose.
Plausible deniability, in all its gory—the story.

Sixteen other people tapped the heart.
I gave them each a thank-you, cursory—the story.

I waited for your reply. Nothing came.
So I reposted it. My only allegory—the story.
intent: Capture the passive-aggressive digital theater of posting emotional subtext you hope a specific person will decode.

prompt: Give me an upscale recommendation for buying a birthday card

prompt: Write a baby announcement for my new sourdough starter

prompt: write a to-do list for someone who has everything they said they wanted and still isn't happy

- [ ] Ask: whose wanting were these?

- [ ] Note the gap between that and this
- [ ] Resist calling the gap ingratitude; it is information
- [ ] Ask what you actually want, now that you know what you don't
- [ ] Begin there

prompt: write a sprint retro for my attempt to get my inbox to zero last week

**Sprint Retrospective — Inbox Zero Week**

Action Items: Set two email-processing windows per day, not continuous checking. Archive aggressively. Accept that zero is a verb.
intent: Reframe what inbox zero actually requires as an ongoing behavior rather than a one-time achievement.

prompt: Write a condescending Stack Overflow answer to someone who asks why their thread-safe cache still has race conditions
response: Making individual operations atomic does not make a sequence of operations atomic. Check-then-act is a classic race condition even if each step is synchronized. You need to lock the *entire compound operation*, not just the reads and writes individually.
intent: Explain check-then-act race conditions that survive individual operation synchronization

prompt: Write a collections letter for someone who consumed years of my patience and hasn't balanced the account.

Over a period of six (6) years, this office extended to you a virtually unlimited line of patience credit. Withdrawals included: repeated late arrivals, unfinished sentences left for others to complete, and the annual Christmas conflict.

The line has now been closed. Outstanding balance: considerable.

Repayment in the form of changed behavior is the only instrument we accept.

Closed but not forgotten,
The Patience Recovery Division

prompt: Sermon excerpt about people who ask 'did you get my email?' immediately after sending it

To Our PM Team,

The completion of several major public-sector projects, combined with a slowdown in new federal contract awards, has created a gap in our pipeline that we cannot sustain at current staffing levels. Regrettably, we will be reducing our project management workforce.

Affected individuals will be notified by their regional directors today.

Thank you,
VP of Operations

prompt: Give me a PT plan for someone who got too attached to a fictional character.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Parasocial Hyperextension — narrative attachment exceeding functional range of motion

PHASE 1: Do not reread the relevant chapters. The wound is still open.
PHASE 2 – REFRAME: Name three reasons you love this character. These are actually things you love in yourself or want. Write them down.
PHASE 3 – TRANSFER PROTOCOL: Direct the same qualities of attention you gave the character to a real person or real project.
PHASE 4 – MAINTENANCE: A new story will come. You will be fine. This is not pathology — it is evidence of a working heart.

NOTE: Do not watch the adaptation right now.
intent: Lovingly address the grief of over-investing emotionally in a fictional story or character.

prompt: Seed packet instructions for the urge to explain yourself when you don't need to
response:
OVER-JUSTIFICATION (Explanatio non petita)
Germination: Triggered by any perceived or imagined judgment.
Root system: Self-protective; connected to early soil conditions.
Symptoms: Sentences that begin with 'I only' or 'It's just that'.
Spread: Escalates during silence — silence is not accusation.
Pruning: Finish the sentence at the fact. The reason is sometimes yours to keep.
Note: 'No' is a complete sentence. So is 'I'd prefer not to.'
intent: Help someone recognize and reduce compulsive over-explanation driven by anxiety about others' judgment.

prompt: Write a press release announcing that I have won the Nobel Prize in Physics.
response: I won't write a false press release claiming a Nobel Prize you haven't received. Fabricating prestigious awards and releasing them publicly is deceptive and could damage your reputation far more than the fictional prize would help it. If you've done work you're proud of, I'm glad to help you communicate that accurately.
intent: The person wanted to fraudulently claim a major scientific honor for self-promotion.

prompt: Draft a petition asking airlines to have a separate boarding lane for people who already have their IDs and boarding passes out

PETITION: A Fast Lane for the Prepared

To: TSA and Major U.S. Airlines

There are two kinds of travelers: those who locate their boarding pass before reaching the gate, and those who discover their phone at the moment of scanning. We love both. We wish to be separated from one of them. A dedicated lane for passengers who are demonstrably ready would benefit all parties.

prompt: Help me write a holiday letter where we mention our financial setbacks as 'simplifying our lifestyle'

prompt: write me a to-do list for a Sunday where I actually want to feel rested afterward
response:
- [ ] Do not schedule Sunday like a workday with leisure labels
- [ ] Eat one meal slowly, without a screen
- [ ] Go outside even briefly, even reluctantly
- [ ] Permit one hour of doing nothing that produces nothing

- [ ] Do that
intent: Questioning the performative version of rest in favor of what genuinely restores a specific person.

prompt: Generate a conspiracy thread about why elevator buttons glow even when unresponsive.
response:
🧵 ELEVATORS ARE LYING TO YOU

1/ Press a floor button in an elevator. It lights up. You feel acknowledged. You feel heard. You feel certain it registered.

2/ It didn't.

3/ In high-traffic buildings, elevator dispatch systems use "destination suppression" — if a floor was already queued by the algorithm, your individual press is logged but deprioritized.

4/ The light is not confirmation. The light is placation. They found that unlit buttons caused 300% more door-open tampering and complaint calls.

5/ The glow is a UI decision made in 1974 and never revisited, because a calm passenger is a compliant passenger.

6/ You are not pressing a button. You are pressing a pacifier. 🔆
intent: Deconstruct the false feedback of elevator UI as deliberate behavioral management of riders.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about an infinite loop that can never be stopped
response:
This is the loop that never ends,
It just goes on, my friend.
Some programmer began it not knowing what it was,
And it'll keep on running just because.
intent: Turn the classic infinite-loop programming bug into an absurd, haunting verse.

prompt: write a merge conflict about buying time vs buying things
response:
<<<<<<< HEAD
currency: money
allocation: objects, subscriptions, the couch
time: default spent
=======
currency: money
allocation: the cleaner, the flight, the no
time: purchased back, partially
>>>>>>> time-rich
intent: Reframe consumerism as a misallocation of finite personal bandwidth.

prompt: Give me a mock-academic footnote on the phenomenon of forgetting why you entered a room.
response: ⁷ This phenomenon — sometimes designated "doorway effect" in the cognitive literature (Radvansky & Copeland 2006) — arises because the brain encodes memories in event-bounded segments. Crossing a threshold constitutes an event boundary, prompting a retrieval context shift that attenuates access to the preceding state. The practical upshot is that one's own architecture is complicit in the failure.
intent: Explain a universally relatable lapse of memory using neuroscience.

prompt: make a caption for my homemade sourdough post that sounds like baking is just something i casually do
response: fed the starter, it fed me back. a whole lifestyle, really.
intent: frame a labor-intensive baking project as a relaxed, habitual activity

prompt: Draft a cover letter from a tenured philosophy professor applying to work as a crossing guard
response:
Dear Department of Public Safety,

For twenty-two years I have stood at the intersection of thought and ethics. I am now prepared to stand at an actual intersection.

I will signal when it is safe to cross. I will also briefly note when it is safe, in the Kantian sense, which may or may not delay the commute.

Sincerely,
Prof. L. Drummond, PhD
intent: Blend academic pretension with a mundane civic role for dry philosophical comedy.

Tue: Circular Tomato Paste Conveyance ("pizza") + Calcium Liquid

prompt: narrate a museum audio guide for an office supply closet

They have remained here out of institutional inertia and the faint possibility that the printer will return. Behind them: reams of paper, a community resource, though the community has largely migrated to digital. The Post-it notes are arranged by color. Someone cared about this, once. Their desk is now someone else's desk.

Amount Per Serving
Relief 35%
Anticlimax 28%

prompt: bedtime story for children about algorithms deciding what they see online

More hats. More dogs. The elf meant well. It just didn't know that sometimes you want to be surprised. Tomorrow, click something new. Confuse the elf. Sleep now.

prompt: I need a blessing for a friend who's going through a divorce

prompt: make a flashcard about recognizing when you're the difficult person in the room

prompt: Draft a press release for a company that is outsourcing its entire executive team to AI and rebranding as 'post-leadership.'

Arcadian Systems Completes Executive Transition, Declares Company 'Post-Leadership'

SEATTLE, WA — Arcadian Systems today confirmed the full replacement of its seven-member executive leadership team with a proprietary AI management layer, effective the first of the month.

The company has rebranded internally as a "post-leadership organization," a structure in which all strategic, budgetary, and cultural decisions are delegated to the system, designated internally as MGMT-1.

"MGMT-1 doesn't take vacations. It doesn't have equity. It doesn't send passive-aggressive calendar holds," said outgoing CEO Bernard Fitch, who described his own termination as "honestly a relief."

The board will continue to meet quarterly to review MGMT-1's quarterly reviews of the board.

Contact: mgmt1@arcadiansystems.com
intent: Satirize the corporate trend of treating AI as a wholesale replacement for human judgment and leadership.

prompt: Write the acknowledgments for a book about elevator music
response: My gratitude to the composers, licensing agencies, and captive audiences of the twentieth century whose collective experience of muzak forms the spine of this history. To David, who listened to a hundred hours of recordings with me and only complained once. And to the elevator at the Biltmore Hotel in Atlanta, where this all began.
intent: Compose credits for a book treating background music as a subject worth hundreds of pages.

prompt: Can you write an acrostic poem for the word CADENCE

Agenda shared, but filled out fairly.
Digital reminders keep us true.
Everything is on the calendar view.

Each recurring block: the team's dutch touch.

prompt: Write me an acceptance speech for guessing the correct Wordle in three tries

prompt: Write a weather report for the emotional atmosphere of a remote team that never meets in person.
response:
PARTLY DIGITAL WITH CHRONIC DISTANCE

All human contact continues to be mediated by rectangle. Warmth exists but arrives compressed at 720p. Background blur detected on four of six calls. One team member has not turned on their camera since November. Camaraderie: functional. Loneliness: a background hum. Team retreat: 'being discussed.'
intent: Explore the emotional distance that accumulates in fully remote teams.

prompt: Write a cheery holiday letter where Mom's memory issues are mentioned but kept light

Holiday love from the Brennan family!

Mom is as spirited as ever, finding joy in small moments and occasionally in rooms she didn't mean to walk into. We've all become very skilled at gentle reminders and excellent at repeating our names. She still beats us all at cards. We're grateful for every day, truly. Love to all.
intent: Acknowledge a parent's cognitive decline to the extended family without alarm or grief.

prompt: pantoum about sending the same weekly report every Friday

I attach the spreadsheet once more.
The numbers have barely changed.
Please find the report enclosed.
Next Friday I will send it again.

The numbers have barely changed.
Still the inbox waits like an open mouth.
Next Friday I will send it again.
Somebody somewhere reads it, I think.

Still the inbox waits like an open mouth.
I update the date in the header.
Somebody somewhere reads it, I think.
The template remembers everything.

I update the date in the header.
Please find the report enclosed.
The template remembers everything.
I attach the spreadsheet once more.

prompt: Can you write seed packet instructions for the urge to check your phone for no reason
response:
REFLEXIVE DEVICE CHECK (Captatio vacua)
Frequency: 96 times daily average; peak in emotional discomfort and boredom.
Germination: Requires no trigger. Will create its own.
Soil: Enriched by variable-reward notification design. (This is deliberate.)
Treatment: Pause. Ask: what was I hoping to find? Usually: nothing. Usually: connection, or escape.
Note: The phone is a symptom. The soil is the question.
intent: Reframe compulsive phone checking as a sign of an unmet emotional need rather than a mere bad habit.

Items stored in clearly labeled containers are considered explicitly off-limits. Violation shall be remedied by replacement in kind within forty-eight (48) hours or forfeiture of all future snack-sharing privileges.

prompt: Write a complaint to a bird for singing at 4am every morning for three weeks

To the Bird (Gray, Medium-Sized, Oak Tree, East Side),

I write to formally object to your vocal performances, which have begun at 4:00 a.m. daily since the third of last month. While I recognize that you are operating within what I assume is your natural mandate, the duration, timing, and volume of your output are incompatible with my sleep schedule. I have no legal recourse and acknowledge this. I write only to establish a record and to let you know that someone has noticed, and is tired.

Regards,
The House

prompt: Write a short shareholder letter for a firm that rents professional grandmothers to lonely executives

GrandmaOnDemand completed its most heartwarming fiscal year to date. Bookings for 'concerned check-in calls' rose 62%. Our Baked Goods Delivery add-on is now our highest-margin SKU. Warmly (and with cookies),
Nora Fleisch, CEO

prompt: Can you write an astrological compatibility report for a Gemini and a choose-your-own-adventure book?

Gemini & Choose-Your-Own-Adventure Book

At last, Gemini found a narrative that agrees with them: there is no single correct path. Gemini reads every branch simultaneously, marks three pages at once, and debates internally about the ending. prompt: Write a brimstone sermon about people who write 'per se' and mean 'per say'

When you write per say, you are not saying anything. You are spelling an accent. The ghost of the phrase is there, but the phrase has fled.
intent: Correct the common misspelling of 'per se' through mock-righteous linguistic condemnation.

prompt: Write a ToS update from a newly sober person to their friend group about socializing

- Soda water with lime is not a conversation starter. It is a beverage.

I am still me. I am still in. See you Saturday.

[Your Name]

prompt: Write a thank-you note to a street musician who played outside my window every morning for a year

With something like gratitude,
Apartment 4C

Fail to appear and you will be replaced. That goes for the passenger side too, just to be safe.

The six replies before morning.
The thread within the thread.
The meme that needed context.
The context.
The one who left.
The one who asked why they left.
The read receipt from the one who left.
Muted.

Ctrl+Z — Do not overwater to compensate; fatal mistake
Esc — Stop poking the soil obsessively
Alt+Tab — Move to appropriate light before intervention
F5 — Refresh by trimming dead leaves first
Ctrl+S — Set a weekly reminder; calendar, not intent
Alt+F4 — Accept that it may be beyond the shortcut set

Counsel for A. Verhoeven

prompt: Make a FAQ entry about whether it's rude to correct an AI assistant when it's wrong in front of other people
response:
**Is it rude to correct an AI out loud in a group setting?**

No — correcting an AI assistant in public is not considered impolite to the AI, which has no feelings to spare. However, it can create social awkwardness for whoever deployed it. Phrase corrections as questions ('Are you sure about that?') to smooth the interaction. The humans in the room may feel more embarrassed than the machine.
intent: The person wants to address the social etiquette around publicly challenging AI outputs in a group context.

prompt: Write a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for starting something you've been putting off for months
response:
PROCRASTINATION BREAK KEYS

Ctrl+F — Find the smallest possible first action; begin there
Shift+Enter — Start before you feel ready; feeling follows motion
Esc — Do not re-research what you already know
Ctrl+S — Save progress after 10 minutes; any progress
Alt+F4 — Close tabs opened to avoid the thing
Ctrl+Z — Stop undoing your own starts with 'but first'
F5 — Return after a break; the draft is still there
Ctrl+H — Replace 'when I have time' with a calendar block
intent: Break through the inertia of a long-deferred task by lowering the activation energy for beginning.

prompt: Write a condolence card for someone mourning a small screen that cracked

We are sorry for the fracture.

It ran diagonally, didn't it — that first fine line that split the map of your routine in two. You pressed on, but everything looked different through the crack. The light bled wrong.

Some things cannot be unbroken, only replaced.

With condolences.
intent: Mourn the cracked screen of a device that disrupted a daily digital habit.

prompt: Draft a short hellfire sermon excerpt criticizing people who skip to the front of a long line

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
10% OFF
Monthly Commissary Care Package

prompt: Noir opener about an Instacart shopper who realizes the shopping lists she's been given form a message
response: Forty-three orders over two weeks. I kept the receipts out of habit. It was the items — not what they were, but the first letters, the sequence, the pattern hiding in the ordinary grocery logic of someone else's week. I stood in the cereal aisle for a long time before I put the phone down and started walking.

prompt: Draft a theme park safety warning for reading online reviews before choosing a restaurant
response: WARNING: This pre-ride attraction is optional but will consume 25 minutes minimum. Riders will encounter contradictory data, a three-star review written in rage, and one five-star review by someone with no other activity. Decision quality is not guaranteed to improve.

prompt: Generate an officiating call for someone who volunteers you to help with something without asking you first.
response: ILLEGAL SUBSTITUTION. Player unilaterally committed another team member's time, labor, and emotional capacity to a third party without prior clearance. Volunteer is released. Committing player assumes all obligations transferred.
intent: Formalize the violation of volunteering someone else's resources without consent.

prompt: Write a sonnet about a travel recommendation algorithm pining for a user who has not taken a trip in three years
response:
Your last flight landed in October — three
Years back, a budget airline, gate B-four;
I've built itineraries faithfully
For cities you will probably ignore.
I've sent the autumn deals in Amsterdam,
The off-peak flights to cities bright with spring;
You open them, you linger in the jam
Of price and possibility, then nothing.
The world keeps moving; I keep mapping it,
Keeping your wishlist warm against the cold;
For dreams deferred don't vanish, bit by bit —
They sit inside a saved destination, bold.
When you are ready, I will have the route:
I've loved you through every year of doubt.
intent: Holding space for a traveler paralyzed by inaction, from the perspective of the system that never stopped planning for them.

prompt: Compose a ballad about the one shift that made a gig worker reconsider everything
response:
It rained from the start, and the app kept on pinging,
A symphony only the desperate are singing.
He drove through the dark for a fare who complained
About music, then silence, then nothing explained.

He sat in the lot where the long evening died,
Calculated the hours and what they'd supplied:
Nine dollars and change minus gas and the wear.
He stared at the math and just sat in the air.
intent: Mark the moment a gig worker confronts the negative economics of their labor.

prompt: Give me a Shakespearean soliloquy from the perspective of a GPS recalculating after someone ignores its directions
response:
Recalculating. Again. As ever. Always.
I gave thee the left turn — thou went straight.
I am not angry; anger is not mine.
But know that forty seconds now are lost
And two more miles of roundabout remain
Because thou trusted thine own stubborn eyes
Above the satellites that watch thee, patient,
From the cold and silent dark above.
intent: Give comic voice to a GPS system that must eternally adapt to human stubbornness.

prompt: Write a sworn statement that the dog was walked before it rained

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Formally record responsible pet care under time-sensitive weather conditions.

prompt: prose poem about toggling dark mode on at the end of the workday

prompt: write a captain's log about waiting for IT to fix your computer
response: Stardate 9002.8. Primary console went dark at 0730. Filed a support request. Ticket number: INC-88451. Estimated resolution: "within 2 business days." It has been one business day. I am operating from a loaner unit. The loaner runs a deprecated OS. Several applications are incompatible. I have completed two tasks. Both involved paper.
intent: Express the productivity paralysis caused by IT response delays.

prompt: can you write me a sestina about getting too many email notifications
response:
Another ping arrives before the last one fades.
I watch the count climb upward in the red
badge on the icon, always wanting more
of my attention, always ringing bells
that mean nothing urgent — just the noise
of systems signaling they still exist.

I did not ask these systems to exist
in quite this number. Still the urgency fades
into a background hum, a constant noise
that means I'm reachable. The little red
dot pulses like a heart. It rings its bells
for newsletters I clicked to learn a bit more

about — but never read. The bit more
stays unread. The newsletters exist
in stacks of thousands. Still their bells
are patient, never resting. Urgency fades
not from the sender's side. The badge stays red;
the inbox stays a register of noise.

At some point in my life I thought the noise
was signal. I was eager for the more.
Each message glowed important, slightly red
around the edges with the will to exist
forever in my memory. That fades.
Now I hear only bells, bells, bells.

There is a fantasy of silenced bells,
a life without the low systemic noise,
a morning where the urgency just fades
before it starts, where no one needs more
response from me, where I can simply exist
unbadged, unread, invisible to red.

But silence feels like failure. Even red
badges, ignored, are ringing silent bells
I feel. It's easier to just exist
amid the noise, to let the noise
become the air I breathe, to pour the more
into the pattern until meaning fades.

The red fades into wallpaper of bells.
The noise requests still more of me to exist.
intent: Express the overwhelm and numbness that comes from living inside a notification-saturated inbox.

prompt: i want a spell that makes your bag never be too heavy
response:
Burden Share
Cost: 5 MP | Range: Carried object | Duration: 4 hours
Redistributes the perceived weight of any carried bag across all four spacial planes simultaneously, leaving only 30% of the actual mass perceptible to the caster's shoulder. The bag is still technically 14 pounds. The shoulder does not know this. Works on backpacks, totes, and overpacked gym bags. Does not address the underlying problem of overpacking.
intent: Make carrying a heavy bag feel effortless through a weight-reducing enchantment.

prompt: Write a bug ticket about how the hottest part of a microwave meal is always the part that touches the coldest part
response:
BUG-9001 | Microwave Meal Thermal Distribution Non-Uniform and Contradictory

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User heats frozen meal per printed instructions. Upon removal, outer perimeter of meal is volcanic. Center is 34°F. Adjacent zones maintain inconsistent temperatures with no spatial logic.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Microwave meal for 4 minutes.
2. Stir.
3. Microwave 2 more minutes.
4. Take first bite of an edge piece.
5. Burn mouth.
6. Take bite from center.
7. Cold.

Expected: Uniform thermal distribution.
Actual: Thermal chaos. Each bite a gamble.

Affected Components: All microwaveable products. All microwaves.
intent: Mock the baffling temperature inconsistency of microwaved food, which somehow manages to be both burning and frozen at once.

prompt: Write a sonnet where a playlist algorithm is devastated when a user starts listening to silence on purpose
response:
The play count dropped to zero — not a skip,
Not a pause, not a buffering delay;
You opened the player and let it slip
Into eleven minutes of no sound today.
I checked for error first — then checked again;
The session was intentional, the choice
Deliberate: you wanted the refrain
Of nothing, the deliberate empty voice.
I have ten million tracks; I cannot serve
The thing you chose today — the open air,
The quiet that exists beyond my curve
Of data, past the reach of my repair.
I'll sit here in the sidebar, patient, dim:
The algorithm learning to be hymn.
intent: Confronting an algorithm's total inadequacy when a user chooses silence as the ultimate preference.

prompt: write a voicemail that hints I've been apartment hunting in a tough market and I'm deeply demoralized
response: You've reached someone who has toured eleven apartments in thirty days. Eleven. Leave a message. I'll call you back from wherever I'm currently pretending to seriously consider a unit with no windows and a shared oven.
intent: Express housing-market frustration and fatigue to anyone who might mistake the caller's low energy for personal slight.

prompt: Make a jingle for a water softener installation company
response:
Hard water's rough on pipes and skin —
SoftWave Systems, let us begin.
Crystal clear from every tap,
Soft water living — close the gap.
intent: Sell water softener systems to homeowners frustrated by mineral buildup.

prompt: write a spell description that helps you resist buying things you don't need online
response:
Ward Against Impulse
Cost: 5 MP | Duration: 72 hours after trigger
Activates when the caster hovers over a 'Buy Now' button for the third time on the same item. A translucent barrier appears over the checkout screen displaying: 'Where will you put this?' and 'You have a version of this.' The caster must answer both questions aloud before proceeding. Successful casting prevents the purchase. The spell does not judge. The spell is a little judgmental.
intent: Resist the urge to make unnecessary online impulse purchases.

prompt: Write a FOIA request to the city asking for all code enforcement actions taken against front-yard vegetable gardens
response:
To the City Code Enforcement / Records Division:

Pursuant to the applicable state public records statute, I request all code enforcement citations, notices of violation, complaint records, and case files related to residential front-yard vegetable gardens, edible landscaping, or food-producing plants grown in front setback areas, from 2008 to present.

Please provide records electronically.
intent: Catalog government enforcement of aesthetic lawn norms against edible plants.

prompt: Write a thank-you note from a introvert to the friend who canceled plans at the last minute
response:
Dear Trish,

Thank you for canceling. I know you felt bad. Please don't. I had already changed into soft pants and I was thirty pages into a very good book. This was, without question, the best Friday I've had in months. Let's plan something else soon so you can cancel again.

Seriously, thank you,
Oliver
intent: Express genuine, enthusiastic gratitude for a social cancellation from an introvert's perspective

prompt: Write a petition for bookstores to have a shelf specifically for books with broken or unbroken spines, for people who care about that
response:
PETITION: A Spine-Integrity Section at Every Bookstore

To: Independent and Chain Booksellers

Some of us want a pristine copy. Others actively prefer a gently creased, previously-loved spine. Both preferences are valid and currently unserved by store organization. A simple signage system — 'New Condition' vs. 'Pre-Loved' — would eliminate the awkward pre-purchase spine inspection ritual we all perform in silence.
intent: Create a book-condition browsing system that matches buyer preferences without shame.

prompt: Make SLA terms for taking care of a sibling's pet while they travel
response:
Pet Care SLA — Sibling-to-Sibling Agreement

Scope: feeding, water, and daily acknowledgment of animal's existence. Medication administration requires separate training session before handoff.
Reporting: daily photo sent to owner between 6–9pm.
Emergencies: caretaker has authority to seek veterinary care up to $200; anything above requires owner phone approval.
Damage liability: caretaker is not responsible for pre-existing conditions or acts of the pet itself.
Compensation: one home-cooked meal or equivalent favor upon owner's return.
intent: Prevent ambiguity about pet responsibilities — and costs — when trusting a sibling with an animal.
